label: AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist
value: Select flaps UP and set propeller speed to 2400 RPM
question: On the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist (minimum 200 ft AAL), what should you do?
distractors: Set flaps T/O and reduce to idle power
Retract flaps and turn electric fuel pump OFF
Extend flaps LDG and set MAX RPM
explanation: Once you are at least 200 ft AAL, the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist calls for flaps UP and 2400 RPM so you transition from takeoff configuration into a clean climb with the prop set for normal climb power.

label: Electric fuel pump OFF timing
question: When should the electric fuel pump be switched OFF after takeoff?
explanation: The electric fuel pump is a backup; above 1000 ft AAL the engine-driven pump should be providing adequate fuel pressure, so the electric pump is switched OFF to avoid unnecessary wear and to confirm the primary system is working.

label: Engine run-up — throttle RPM (brakes held)
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off
question: During the engine run-up, to what RPM is the throttle set with the brakes held?
explanation: 1700 RPM is the standard run-up power with brakes held — high enough to check magnetos, prop, and carb heat, but low enough to keep the aircraft stationary and manageable on the ground.

label: Maximum delay before shutting down the engine
question: If oil pressure is below 1.5 bar after start, what is the maximum delay before shutting down the engine?
explanation: The CAUTION on engine start limits how long you may wait for oil pressure: if it stays below 1.5 bar, shut down within 10 seconds to avoid running the engine without proper lubrication.

label: DOWNWIND checklist
question: On the DOWNWIND checklist, what position should carburetor heat be in?
explanation: Carburetor heat ON on downwind prevents carburetor icing during the prolonged low-power phase of the circuit, when the throttle is often well below 20" MP.

label: Target speed on the BALKED LANDING checklist
question: What is the target speed on the BALKED LANDING checklist?
explanation: 58 kts is Vx with flaps T/O — the best angle of climb speed used on a balked landing to gain altitude quickly while obstacles may still be a factor.

label: Initial airspeed on the ENGINE FAILURE DURING FLIGHT abnormal checklist
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine failure during flight
question: What is the initial airspeed on the ENGINE FAILURE DURING FLIGHT abnormal checklist?
explanation: 70 kts is the initial target after engine failure in cruise — it matches best glide with flaps UP and gives you time to fly the airplane and plan your landing before further configuration changes.

label: Emergency landing with engine off — approach speed
source: Abnormal checklist - Emergency landing with engine off
question: What airspeed does the EMERGENCY LANDING WITH ENGINE OFF checklist specify?
explanation: 59 kts is the specified approach speed for an emergency landing with the engine off, balancing controllability with minimum sink in the landing configuration.

label: Flap system failure (UP only) — approach speed adjustment
question: In a flap system failure where only the UP position is available, how should the approach speed be adjusted?
explanation: With flaps stuck UP you lose the drag and lift benefit of landing flaps, so approach speed is raised by 10 kts to maintain adequate control margin above the higher stall speed.

label: Taxi warm-up — oil temperature RPM
question: During taxi warm-up, at what RPM is the engine oil warmed to 50 C?
explanation: During cold taxi warm-up, 1100–1500 RPM is enough to bring engine oil to 50 °C without excessive brake loading or overheating on the ground.

label: Vy (best rate of climb)
question: What is Vy (best rate of climb) for the DV20?
explanation: Vy of 70 kts with flaps UP is the speed that produces the greatest rate of climb in the clean configuration — more altitude per minute than Vx, which optimizes angle instead.

label: Vy (best rate of climb) with flaps in the T/O position
question: What is Vy (best rate of climb) with flaps in the T/O position?
explanation: With takeoff flaps still selected, Vy drops to 65 kts because the wing produces more lift at a lower angle of attack, changing the best rate-of-climb point.

label: Vx (best angle of climb) with flaps T/O
question: What is Vx (best angle of climb) with flaps T/O?
explanation: Vx of 58 kts with flaps T/O gives the steepest climb path — use it when you need to clear an obstacle rather than maximize climb rate.

label: Stall speed VS1 (flaps up)
question: What is the stall speed VS1 (flaps up)?
explanation: VS1 of 43 kts is the clean-configuration stall speed (flaps UP) — the baseline reference for approach margins and slow-flight training.

label: VSO (stall speed, flaps down)
question: What is VSO (stall speed, flaps down)?
explanation: VSO of 38 kts is the stall speed with landing flaps — the lower end of the white arc on the airspeed indicator.

label: VFE (maximum speed with flaps extended)
question: What is VFE (maximum speed with flaps extended)?
explanation: VFE of 81 kts is the maximum speed with flaps extended; exceeding it risks structural damage to the flap system, which is why flap changes require a "Speed checked" callout below this limit.

label: VA (maneuvering speed)
question: What is VA (maneuvering speed)?
explanation: VA of 104 kts is maneuvering speed — below this speed the wing will stall before exceeding the design load limit in full-deflection maneuvers.

label: Maximum demonstrated crosswind component
question: What is the maximum demonstrated crosswind component?
explanation: 15 kts is the maximum demonstrated crosswind component; it represents flight-test experience, not a hard legal limit, but is the figure you plan around for safe operations.

label: Best glide speed with flaps UP
question: What is the best glide speed with flaps UP?
explanation: Best glide with flaps UP is 70 kts — the speed that maximizes range and time aloft after an engine failure in the clean configuration.

label: Best glide speed with flaps T/O
question: What is the best glide speed with flaps T/O?
explanation: With takeoff flaps out, best glide is 64 kts — slightly slower than clean because the extended flaps change the wing's lift/drag characteristics.

label: Maximum takeoff weight for PH-MFT and PH-SKM
question: What is the maximum takeoff weight for PH-MFT and PH-SKM?
explanation: 730 kg is the maximum takeoff weight for PH-MFT and PH-SKM; exceeding it violates loading limits and degrades performance margins.

label: Usable fuel tank capacity
source: General aircraft numbers - Average fuel usage (p.17)
question: What is the usable fuel tank capacity?
explanation: 77 liters is the usable fuel capacity — the quantity you can actually burn in flight, distinct from total tank capacity which includes unusable fuel.

label: Approximate glide ratio (L/Dmax) of the Katana
question: What is the approximate glide ratio (L/Dmax) of the Katana?
explanation: A 14:1 glide ratio means for every 1000 ft of altitude you can glide roughly 2.8 nm in still air — a planning figure for engine-out scenarios.

label: Standard power setting for straight and level flight
source: Normal procedures - Straight and level flight (p.23)
question: What is the standard power setting for straight and level flight?
explanation: 25" MP, 2400 RPM, and 90 kts is the standard straight-and-level cruise setting — a balanced power configuration for efficient level flight per the normal procedures.

label: Power/speed table — cruise (flaps UP)
question: What does the power/speed table specify for cruise (flaps UP)?
explanation: The power/speed table lists ±25" MP, 2400 RPM, and 90 kts for cruise with flaps UP — the reference setting when established in level flight.

label: Power/speed table — descent (flaps UP)
question: What does the power/speed table specify for descent (flaps UP)?
explanation: For descent with flaps UP, the table specifies ±18" MP at 2400 RPM and 90 kts — reduced power while maintaining airspeed for a controlled descent.

label: Downwind flaps T/O — throttle setting
question: On downwind, after selecting flaps T/O, to what setting is the throttle advanced?
explanation: Selecting flaps T/O on downwind increases drag; advancing to ±24" MP restores the energy needed to hold 70 kts in the circuit.

label: Initial throttle (MP) setting when turning base
question: What is the initial throttle (MP) setting when turning base?
explanation: 15" MP on base is the initial power reduction that begins slowing the aircraft from downwind speed toward final approach speed.

label: Minimum approach speed on final
source: Powersetting table / Circuit and normal landing (p.18, 39)
question: What is the minimum approach speed on final?
explanation: 60 kts is the minimum approach speed on final — the floor that keeps adequate margin above stall while allowing a stabilized approach.

label: Minimum power setting requiring carburetor heat ON
question: Below what power setting (MP) must carburetor heat be ON?
explanation: Below 20" MP the throttle is low enough that carburetor icing risk rises significantly, so carburetor heat must be ON per the power table note.

label: Power/speed table — climb speed (flaps T/O)
value: 65 kts at full throttle, PSCL full forward
question: Per the power/speed table, what is the target speed for climb with flaps T/O?
explanation: Climb with flaps T/O uses full throttle and prop full forward at 65 kts — matching Vy for the takeoff flap configuration on the power/speed table.

label: Descent — extra rate per inch MP reduction
question: During descent, approximately how much extra descent rate does each inch of reduced manifold pressure give?
explanation: Each inch of reduced manifold pressure adds roughly 100 ft/min of descent rate — a useful rule of thumb when fine-tuning your descent profile.

label: Circuit continuation — power and speed at 700 ft AAL
question: When staying in the circuit, what power setting and speed are set at 700 ft AAL?
explanation: At 700 ft AAL in the circuit, ±21" MP and 70 kts is the standard downwind configuration — enough power to maintain circuit speed with flaps still UP.

label: Transition to climb — full throttle speed
question: In the transition from straight and level to climb, at what speed is the throttle advanced to FULL?
explanation: Advancing to full throttle at 70 kts during the climb transition ensures you have adequate airspeed before adding climb power, preventing excessive pitch and stall risk.

label: Level-off from climb — power reduction speed
source: Normal procedures - Transition from climb to straight and level (p.24)
question: When leveling off from a climb, at what airspeed is power reduced to 25"/2400 RPM?
explanation: At 90 kts during level-off from climb, power is reduced to 25"/2400 RPM — the standard cruise setting once the aircraft is stabilized in level flight.

label: Transition to descent — carburetor heat
question: In the transition to descent, what should be done with carburetor heat?
explanation: Carburetor heat goes ON before reducing power for descent because the sudden power reduction creates ideal conditions for carburetor icing.

label: Level-off from descent — carburetor heat OFF point
source: Normal procedures - Transition from descent to straight and level (p.24)
question: When leveling off from a descent, at what point is carburetor heat turned OFF?
explanation: Turning carb heat OFF 100 ft before the target altitude during a descent level-off prevents carrying unnecessary heat into the cruise power setting.

label: Standard bank angle for a level turn
question: What is the standard bank angle for a level turn?
explanation: 30° bank is the standard for level turns — steep enough for reasonable turn rate while keeping g-load and stall speed increase manageable.

label: Approximate target speed during a 30 degree level turn
question: What is the approximate target speed during a 30 degree level turn?
explanation: In a 30° level turn, target approximately 95 kts to compensate for the increased load factor and prevent altitude loss.

label: Maximum bank angle for a climbing turn
question: What is the maximum bank angle for a climbing turn?
explanation: Maximum 15° bank in a climbing turn limits the additional stall-speed rise and helps maintain Vy while turning — steeper banks bleed climb performance.

label: Steep turn — power increase at 30° bank
question: During a 45 degree steep turn, what should you do when passing 30 degrees of bank?
explanation: At 30° bank in a steep turn, increasing power by 2" compensates for the extra induced drag and load factor that would otherwise cause altitude loss.

label: Minimum altitude for the steep turn exercise when solo
question: What is the minimum altitude for the steep turn exercise when solo?
explanation: 2000 ft AGL minimum for solo steep turns provides recovery altitude if something goes wrong during the maneuver.

label: Scanflow — traffic reporting method
value: Clock position combined with above/below/on the horizon
source: Normal procedures - Reporting of other airplanes (p.20)
question: What method is recommended for reporting traffic during the scanflow?
explanation: Clock position plus above/below/on the horizon gives other traffic a precise, unambiguous location that both pilots can visualize quickly.

label: Clearing turns — target speed
question: What speed should be maintained during clearing turns before maneuvers?
explanation: 90 kts during clearing turns keeps the aircraft stable and gives good visibility while scanning before maneuvers.

label: Target speed when establishing a glide from straight and level
question: What is the target speed when establishing a glide from straight and level?
explanation: 70 kts is the target glide speed from straight and level — matching best glide with flaps UP for a controlled transition into engine-idle flight.

label: Slow flight target speed
question: At what speed is slow flight performed?
explanation: Slow flight is flown at VS + 10 kts — close enough to the stall to be realistic, but with a deliberate margin for control and recovery practice.

label: Slow flight entry — initial power reduction
question: On entry to slow flight, to what power setting is power initially reduced?
explanation: Reducing to 12–15" MP on slow-flight entry bleeds energy gradually so you can stabilize at VS+10 without overshooting into a stall.

label: Approach-to-stall — recovery trigger (besides warning/buffet)
question: Besides stall warning and buffet, what is the other approach-to-stall recovery trigger?
explanation: Recovery triggers include the stall warning, buffet, or reaching 5 kts above stall speed — whichever comes first ensures you recover before a full stall develops.

label: Power-on stall recovery — minimum speed before climb attitude
question: In a power-on stall recovery, what is the minimum speed before raising the nose to climb attitude?
explanation: In a power-on stall recovery, 60 kts minimum before raising the nose ensures adequate airflow and control authority to safely transition back to climb attitude.

label: Minimum recovery altitude for a full stall when solo
question: What is the minimum recovery altitude for a full stall when solo?
explanation: 3000 ft AGL minimum for solo full stalls provides enough altitude to recover from a developed stall and any altitude loss during the maneuver.

label: Minimum recovery altitude for an approach to stall when solo
question: What is the minimum recovery altitude for an approach to stall when solo?
explanation: 2000 ft AGL minimum for solo approach-to-stall exercises allows recovery before reaching an unsafe altitude, with less margin needed than a full stall.

label: Stall recovery — priority action
value: Decrease angle of attack, not altitude loss
question: During stall recovery, what takes priority?
explanation: Stall recovery always prioritizes reducing angle of attack — trying to save altitude before unstalling the wing can deepen the stall and worsen the situation.

label: Before lowering flaps — required action
value: Check speed is in the white arc and call out "Speed checked"
source: Normal procedures - Slow flight / stall (white arc call-out)
question: What must you do before lowering the flaps?
explanation: The white arc callout confirms your speed is at or below VFE before any flap change, preventing structural damage from extending flaps at too high an airspeed.

label: Accelerating from slow flight — flap retraction speed
source: Normal procedures - Slow flight, accelerate to 90 kts (p.29)
question: When accelerating out of slow flight with flaps, by what speed must the flaps be retracted?
explanation: Flaps must be fully retracted by 81 kts (VFE) when accelerating out of slow flight — you cannot leave flaps down above the maximum flap-extended speed.

label: Standard circuit altitude (uncontrolled)
question: At what altitude is the standard traffic circuit flown at uncontrolled aerodromes?
explanation: 700 ft AAL is the standard circuit height at uncontrolled aerodromes — low enough for efficient operations, high enough for a stable pattern.

label: Circuit area vertical extent
question: Up to what altitude does the aerodrome circuit area extend vertically?
explanation: The circuit area extends vertically to 1000 ft AAL — traffic above this height is outside the standard circuit protection volume.

label: Standard circuit join point and angle
question: Where and at what angle should you join the standard circuit?
explanation: Joining at half-way downwind on a 90° intercept gives a predictable, non-conflicting entry that keeps you aligned with standard circuit flow.

label: Circuit departure point
question: Where does leaving the aerodrome circuit normally take place?
explanation: Leaving the circuit at the 45° half-way crosswind point keeps your departure clear of downwind traffic and follows standard VFR circuit procedure.

label: Downwind HARS check — included speed
source: Landing - Circuit and normal landing, HARS (p.39)
question: What speed is included in the downwind HARS check?
explanation: 70 kts is the downwind speed checked in HARS — the reference circuit speed before flap selection and base turn.

label: Turning base — threshold wing position
question: When turning base, where should the threshold be relative to the wing?
explanation: With the threshold 45–60° behind the wing on base, you are at the correct position and distance for a stable base-to-final turn.

label: Base-to-final turn — altitude and position
question: At approximately what altitude and position is the base-to-final turn made?
explanation: Turning base-to-final at 500 ft and 20° before the extended centerline sets up a stabilized final without overshooting or cutting in too close.

label: Final centerline — established altitude
question: By what altitude should you be established on the final centerline?
explanation: Being established on final centerline by 300 ft gives enough time to stabilize speed and glidepath before the roundout.

label: Final approach — max flap extension speed
question: Below what speed may the flaps be selected?
explanation: Flaps may only be selected below 81 kts (VFE) — the structural limit for extended flaps on the DV20.

label: Minimum height for overflying a circuit area to read the signal area
question: What is the minimum height for overflying a circuit area to read the signal area?
explanation: Overflying the circuit at a minimum of 1000 ft AAL keeps you clear of circuit traffic while allowing a safe read of the signal area.

label: Rotation speed for a normal takeoff
question: What is the rotation speed for a normal takeoff?
explanation: 51 kts is the normal rotation speed — the point where the wing generates enough lift to leave the ground smoothly without premature rotation.

label: Normal takeoff — post-rotation acceleration speed
value: 65 kts (Vy flaps T/O) then 70 kts after flap retraction
question: After rotation on a normal takeoff, to what speed do you accelerate?
explanation: After rotation, accelerate to 65 kts (Vy with T/O flaps) then 70 kts after flap retraction — the standard climb speed sequence for a normal takeoff.

label: Normal takeoff (long hard runway) — flap setting
question: For a normal takeoff from a long, hard-surface runway, which flap setting is used?
explanation: Flaps UP on a long hard runway minimizes drag during the takeoff roll, shortening ground run when runway length is not a constraint.

label: Normal takeoff (short/soft runway) — flap setting
question: For a normal takeoff from a short runway (<800 m) or a soft/grass surface, which flap setting is used?
explanation: Flaps T/O on short (<800 m) or soft/grass runways reduces the rotation and liftoff speed, shortening ground run and improving takeoff performance.

label: Rotation speed for an obstacle clearance (short field) takeoff
question: What is the rotation speed for an obstacle clearance (short field) takeoff?
explanation: 57 kts rotation on an obstacle clearance takeoff is slightly higher than normal to ensure positive climb performance over obstacles.

label: Obstacle clearance takeoff — climb speed
question: What climb speed is held until clear of obstacles on an obstacle clearance takeoff?
explanation: 58 kts (Vx) after an obstacle clearance takeoff maximizes climb angle until obstacles are cleared, then you can accelerate to Vy.

label: Soft/rough field takeoff — nose-raise speed
source: Takeoff procedures - Soft or rough field takeoff (p.37)
question: On a soft/rough field takeoff, at what speed do you gently raise the nose to climb attitude?
explanation: Raising the nose gently at 60 kts on a soft field takeoff lifts the nosewheel early to reduce drag and prevent the nosewheel from digging in.

label: Takeoff roll
question: During the takeoff roll, what should the engine instruments read?
explanation: 2400 RPM ±100 during the takeoff roll confirms the engine is delivering full rated power — a critical check before committing to flight.

label: Electric fuel pump when leaving the circuit
source: Takeoff procedures - Normal takeoff, leaving the circuit (p.36)
question: When leaving the circuit after departure, what is done with the electric fuel pump?
explanation: The electric fuel pump is switched OFF when leaving the circuit because the engine-driven pump is now providing adequate fuel pressure at cruise power.

label: Normal minimum speed over the threshold
source: Landing - Circuit and normal landing, NOTE (p.39)
question: What is the normal minimum speed over the threshold?
explanation: 60 kts minimum over the threshold on a normal landing keeps adequate stall margin while allowing a stabilized approach in the landing configuration.

label: Minimum threshold speed for a short field landing
question: What is the minimum threshold speed for a short field landing?
explanation: 55 kts minimum threshold speed on a short field landing reduces float and ground roll, maximizing available runway.

label: Flaps-up landing — circuit speeds
question: In a flaps-up landing, what are the downwind, base, and final speeds?
explanation: A flaps-up landing uses 70 kts throughout the circuit because without flaps the higher stall speed requires a faster approach profile.

label: Normal landing — throttle idle point
value: Level flight about 1 m above the runway is established
question: During a normal landing, when is the throttle retarded to idle?
distractors: During the round-out before the threshold
explanation: Retarding the throttle to idle once level flight about 1 m above the runway is established in the roundout — the standard technique for a normal landing.

label: Soft field landing technique
value: Keep nosewheel off as long as possible with full back pressure
question: During soft field landing, what touchdown technique should you emphasize?
distractors: Brake immediately after main gear touchdown
explanation: Keeping the nosewheel off with full back pressure on a soft field landing prevents the nosewheel from digging into soft surface and damaging the aircraft.

label: Short field landing — post-nosewheel action
question: On a short field landing, what is done after the nosewheel touches down?
distractors: Leave flaps LDG until clear of runway
explanation: After a short field landing, flaps UP reduces drag for braking effectiveness, and careful braking minimizes ground roll without risking tire damage.

label: Touch and go (paved) — flap setting
question: What flap setting is used for a touch and go on a paved runway?
explanation: Flaps UP for a touch and go on pavement minimizes drag on the subsequent takeoff roll when runway length allows.

label: Touch and go (grass) — flap setting
question: What flap setting is used for a touch and go on grass?
explanation: Flaps T/O for a touch and go on grass improves takeoff performance on the softer surface where reduced ground run matters.

label: Strong crosswind — recommended landing type
question: In a strong crosswind, what type of landing may be advisable?
explanation: A flapless landing in strong crosswind reduces the side force on the flaps and gives better roll control during the landing roll.

label: First actions in a go-around / balked landing
value: MAX RPM, throttle FULL, carburetor heat OFF
question: What are the first actions in a go-around / balked landing?
distractors: Flaps UP, idle power, carb heat ON
Retract all flaps, then add power
explanation: MAX RPM, full throttle, and carb heat OFF are the immediate go-around actions — restoring full climb power without the power loss carb heat causes.

label: Go-around — flap setting after power
value: T/O after "Speed checked" within white arc
question: After applying power in a go-around, what flap setting is selected?
explanation: T/O flaps after "Speed checked" within the white arc gives partial flap for climb while staying below VFE during the initial go-around acceleration.

label: Initial climb speed sequence after a go-around
value: 58 kts (Vx), then 65 kts (Vy) when clear of obstacles
question: What is the initial climb speed sequence after a go-around?
explanation: 58 kts (Vx) initially after go-around maximizes climb angle; transition to 65 kts (Vy) once clear of obstacles for best rate of climb.

label: Go-around — flaps UP height/prop setting
question: After a go-around, above what height are flaps selected UP and the prop set to 2400 RPM?
explanation: Above 200 ft AAL after go-around, flaps UP and 2400 RPM transition the aircraft into the standard climb configuration.

label: Go-around at Hilversum — runway side restriction
value: Right side of runway due to gliders/paras
question: During a go-around at Hilversum, which side of the runway may you need to stay clear of?
explanation: At Hilversum, staying clear of the right side of the runway on go-around avoids conflict with gliders and paragliders operating from that side.

label: Rejected takeoff — abort criterion
question: A rejected takeoff should be executed if a continued takeoff becomes unsafe before what point?
explanation: A rejected takeoff must be executed before rotation — once airborne, abort options are severely limited and the procedure changes entirely.

label: Minimum altitude to initiate simulated EFATO
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: Below what height should a simulated engine failure after takeoff (EFATO) exercise not be initiated?
explanation: EFATO simulation below 300 ft AGL is prohibited because there is insufficient altitude to safely practice the maneuver and recover.

label: Engine failure after takeoff — glide speed
value: 59 kts with flaps T/O, or 70 kts with flaps UP
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: After an engine failure after takeoff, to what glide speed do you lower the nose?
explanation: Glide speed after takeoff engine failure depends on flap setting: 59 kts with T/O flaps, 70 kts with flaps UP — matching the configuration-specific best glide speeds.

label: Insufficient engine power drill — carburetor heat position
question: On the insufficient engine power drill, what position is carburetor heat set to?
explanation: Carb heat OFF on the insufficient power drill eliminates carb icing as a cause and ensures full power delivery while troubleshooting.

label: Engine failure in flight (>1500 ft) — fly-to area
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: On an engine failure in flight above 1500 ft, where do you fly?
explanation: Above 1500 ft, fly a downwind with a 1000 ft abeam point — enough altitude to set up a normal circuit approach to a landing site.

label: Engine failure in flight (<700 ft) — fly-to area
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: On an engine failure in flight below 700 ft, where do you fly?
explanation: Below 700 ft, fly direct final — there is insufficient altitude for a full circuit, so the most direct approach to a landing site is required.

label: Engine failure in flight — transponder code
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: What transponder code is set during an engine failure in flight?
explanation: 7700 is the transponder code for emergency — alerting ATC and other traffic that you have an urgent situation.

label: Emergency landing engine off — approach speed
source: Abnormal checklist / Engine failure during flight (p.45-46)
question: What airspeed is used for an emergency landing with the engine off?
explanation: 59 kts for emergency landing with engine off matches the checklist approach speed for a controlled descent to touchdown with flaps in landing configuration.

label: First action for an electrical fire (with smoke) in flight
source: Abnormal checklist - Electrical fire including smoke in flight
question: What is the first action for an electrical fire (with smoke) in flight?
explanation: Master switch OFF is the first action for electrical fire — removing the power source stops the current that is feeding the fire.

label: Generator warning light with discharge — action
value: Switch off all nonessential equipment and land at nearest suitable airport
source: Abnormal checklist - Generator warning light with engine running
question: If the generator warning light shows with discharge on the ammeter, what should you do?
distractors: Turn master switch ON and OFF repeatedly
explanation: With generator failure, shedding nonessential loads reduces electrical demand and may allow the remaining battery capacity to reach a suitable airport.

label: Engine fire in flight — checklist airspeed
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire in flight
question: What airspeed does the ENGINE FIRE IN FLIGHT checklist specify?
explanation: 70 kts during engine fire in flight maintains control while minimizing airflow that could feed the fire, per the abnormal checklist.

label: First priority in all abnormal procedures
value: Maintain airplane control — fly the airplane
question: What is the first priority in all abnormal procedures?
explanation: Fly the airplane first — maintaining control is always the top priority in any abnormal situation before troubleshooting or running checklists.

label: Fuel dipstick — lowest notch indication
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick (p.7)
question: On the fuel dipstick, what does the lowest notch indicate?
explanation: The lowest notch on the fuel dipstick marks 10 liters of usable fuel — the minimum quantity reading you should expect from a dip check.

label: Preferred approved fuel at EHHV
source: General Aircraft Information - Approved fuel grades (p.16)
question: What is the preferred approved fuel at EHHV?
explanation: MOGAS per BAZ-Regulation is the preferred fuel at EHHV (Hilversum), reflecting local operating approval for automotive gasoline.

label: Approved lubricant CAUTION — prohibited substitute
source: General Aircraft Information - Approved lubricant, CAUTION (p.16)
question: According to the document's CAUTION, what must NOT be used as a lubricant?
distractors: SF or SG marked automotive oil
explanation: Aviation lubricant must never substitute for engine oil — it lacks the properties required for engine lubrication and can cause serious engine damage.

label: Departure briefing — Hilversum circuit join point
question: In the departure briefing example, where is the circuit left at Hilversum?
explanation: The example departure briefing leaves the circuit at 700 ft on the crosswind leg under 45° — the standard Hilversum departure procedure.

label: Threat and error management — briefing purpose
value: Threats that affect intended operations and how to mitigate them
question: What should threat and error management briefings identify?
explanation: TEM briefings identify threats to your operation and how to mitigate them — turning potential problems into planned responses before they occur.

label: EHHV glide-in — downwind requirement
value: Mandatory call, no traffic ahead, instructor only
question: What does an EHHV glide-in from 1000 ft downwind require?
explanation: The EHHV glide-in from 1000 ft downwind requires a mandatory call, no traffic ahead, and instructor only — it is not approved for solo students.

label: Solo student call sign prefix
question: What call sign prefix should solo student pilots use?
explanation: Solo student pilots prefix their call sign with STUDENT so other traffic and ATC recognize they are a student and may need additional consideration.

label: Instruments/brakes check timing during taxi
source: Normal procedures - Instruments/brakes check during taxiing (p.19)
question: When taxiing, the instruments/brakes check is called out after leaving what?
explanation: The instruments/brakes check is called out after leaving the parking area — confirming systems are working before entering active taxi routes.

label: VNE (never exceed speed)
question: What is VNE (never exceed speed) for the DV20?
explanation: VNE of 161 kts is the never-exceed speed — the red line on the airspeed indicator that must never be exceeded in any condition.

label: VNO (maximum structural cruising speed)
question: What is VNO (maximum structural cruising speed)?
explanation: VNO of 117 kts is the maximum structural cruising speed in smooth air — above this, only fly in smooth conditions or reduce speed.

label: Maximum baggage allowance
question: What is the maximum baggage allowance for the DV20?
explanation: 20 kg maximum baggage in the aft compartment keeps the CG within limits and prevents overloading the rear of the aircraft.

label: Empty weight PH-MFT
question: What is the empty weight of PH-MFT?
explanation: 511 kg is the empty weight of PH-MFT — the baseline for weight and balance calculations before adding fuel, occupants, and baggage.

label: Maximum G-load with flaps extended
question: What is the maximum G-load range with flaps extended (normal category)?
explanation: With flaps extended, the normal category G-load range is 0.0G to +2.0G — reduced from the clean configuration due to the lower structural limit with flaps down.

label: Circuit speed with flaps UP
question: Per the power/speed table, what speed is flown in the circuit with flaps UP?
explanation: The circuit with flaps UP uses 75 kts at ±21" MP per the power table — slightly faster than the 70 kts used with T/O flaps because clean configuration has a higher stall speed.

label: Engine run-up — propeller RPM drop check
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: During the engine run-up propeller check, what RPM drop is expected?
explanation: A 100–200 RPM drop during the propeller check confirms the governor is functioning and can control prop pitch at run-up RPM.

label: Engine run-up — magneto check limits
value: Max 150 RPM drop, max 50 RPM difference between L and R
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: During the magneto check at 1500 RPM, what are the acceptable RPM drop limits?
distractors: Max 30 RPM drop, max 100 RPM difference
Max 200 RPM drop, max 50 RPM difference
No drop allowed on either magneto
explanation: Mag check limits of max 150 RPM drop per side and max 50 RPM difference between L and R confirm both magnetos and the ignition system are healthy.

label: Carburetor heat check — expected RPM drop
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: During the carburetor heat check at 1500 RPM, what RPM drop confirms carb heat is working?
explanation: A 30 RPM drop during the carb heat check confirms heated air is reaching the carburetor — proving the carb heat system works before flight.

label: AFTER LANDING checklist — flap position
question: On the AFTER LANDING checklist (clear of runway), what flap position is selected?
explanation: Flaps UP on the AFTER LANDING checklist reduces drag and prepares the aircraft for taxi — flaps are not needed on the ground.

label: AFTER LANDING checklist — carburetor heat
question: On the AFTER LANDING checklist, what position is carburetor heat set to?
explanation: Carb heat OFF after landing because full power is no longer needed and carb icing risk is minimal during taxi with intermittent power changes.

label: Fuel gauge at ¼ indication — action required
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick and fuel gauge (p.7)
question: When the fuel gauge indicates ¼ tank, what action does the procedures document require?
distractors: Continue flight for up to 1 hour
Switch to electric fuel pump only
explanation: At ¼ tank on the fuel gauge, land within 30 minutes — the gauge reads optimistically, so this is a conservative fuel reserve trigger.

label: Level-off from climb — when to lower the nose
source: Normal procedures - Transition from climb to straight and level (p.24)
question: When leveling off from a climb, how far before the desired altitude do you begin lowering the nose?
explanation: Begin lowering the nose 20 ft before the target altitude during climb level-off — a small lead that prevents overshooting the assigned level.

label: Transition from glide to level — carb heat and power
source: Normal procedures - Transition from glide to straight and level (p.26)
question: When transitioning from a glide to straight and level, at what point do you turn carburetor heat OFF and increase power?
explanation: 150 ft before target altitude during glide level-off gives time to add power and turn carb heat OFF smoothly without an altitude overshoot.

label: Engine clearing during a glide — duration at full power
source: Normal procedures - Clearing the engine during a glide (p.25)
question: When clearing the engine during a glide, how long is full power applied before returning to idle?
explanation: 3–5 seconds of full power during a prolonged glide clears spark plug fouling from the rich mixture at idle — keeping the engine ready for a power addition.

label: Scanflow — engine instruments check interval
question: During level flight scanflow, how often should engine instruments and fuel quantity be checked?
explanation: Engine instruments and fuel quantity checked every 15 minutes during cruise catches developing problems early while they are still manageable.

label: Descending turn — standard bank angle
question: What bank angle is used for a descending turn?
explanation: 30° bank in a descending turn matches the standard level turn bank angle while allowing a controlled descent path.

label: Slow flight — minimum solo altitude
question: What is the minimum altitude for slow flight when flying solo?
explanation: 2000 ft AGL minimum for solo slow flight provides recovery margin if control degrades near the stall.

label: Slow flight — power setting at VS+10
question: In slow flight at VS+10, what power setting maintains the maneuver?
explanation: 22–24" MP maintains slow flight at VS+10 — enough power to hold altitude near the stall without accelerating away from the maneuver.

label: Full stall — minimum solo recovery altitude
question: What is the minimum recovery altitude for a full stall exercise when solo?
explanation: 3000 ft AGL minimum for solo full stalls — same as the general full-stall solo minimum, providing adequate recovery altitude.

label: Overtaking within the traffic circuit
question: Is overtaking other aircraft permitted within the aerodrome traffic circuit?
distractors: Permitted on the downwind leg only
Permitted if faster aircraft announces on radio
explanation: Overtaking within the circuit is not permitted — it disrupts standard circuit spacing and creates collision risk in a confined traffic pattern.

label: Flap retraction after takeoff — minimum height
question: At what minimum height are flaps selected UP after a normal takeoff?
explanation: Flaps UP at minimum 200 ft AAL after takeoff follows the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist — retracting flaps once safely airborne and clear of obstacles.

label: Short runway threshold for flaps T/O
question: Below what runway length should flaps T/O be used for a normal takeoff?
explanation: Below 800 m runway length, use flaps T/O for takeoff — the shorter ground run from the lower liftoff speed is essential on limited runway.

label: Engine failure in flight (700–1500 ft) — fly-to area
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: On an engine failure in flight between 700 ft and 1500 ft, where do you fly?
explanation: Between 700 and 1500 ft after engine failure, fly direct base — enough altitude for a base leg but not a full downwind circuit.

label: Rejected takeoff — initial callout
question: What should you call out when executing a rejected takeoff?
explanation: Calling STOP during a rejected takeoff alerts your instructor/passenger and confirms your decision to abort — a standard callout for a RTO.

label: EFATO — landing site selection angle
value: 30° left or right from the nose
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: After an engine failure after takeoff, within what angular range from the nose should you choose a landing site?
explanation: Choose a landing site within 30° left or right of the nose after EFATO — you cannot turn back, so the field must be within your forward arc.

label: Emergency landing — crash drill airspeed
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight, crash drill (p.47)
question: During the crash drill for an emergency landing, what airspeed is maintained?
explanation: 59 kts during the crash drill on final matches the emergency approach speed — practicing the speed you would fly on an actual forced landing.

label: Loss of fuel pressure — first action
source: Abnormal checklist - Loss of fuel pressure (p.10)
question: If the fuel pressure warning light illuminates, what is the first checklist action?
explanation: Electric fuel pump ON is the first action for loss of fuel pressure — the backup pump restores fuel flow while you diagnose the cause.

label: Engine fire in flight — flap setting
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire in flight (p.10)
question: On the ENGINE FIRE IN FLIGHT checklist, what flap setting is specified?
explanation: T/O flaps during engine fire in flight provide partial flap for a controlled approach speed without the full drag of landing flaps.

label: Normal landing — glidepath angle
question: On a normal landing, what glidepath angle should the aircraft be positioned on?
explanation: A 3° glidepath on final is the standard approach angle — it provides a stabilized descent profile to the threshold.

label: Precautionary landing — inspection run ground speed timing at 70 kts
source: Precautionary landing - Inspection run timing table (p.50)
question: During a precautionary landing inspection run at 70 kts ground speed, what timing corresponds to the minimal landing distance check?
explanation: 14 seconds at 70 kts ground speed during a precautionary landing inspection run corresponds to the minimal landing distance check per the timing table.

label: Go-around — initial callout
question: What should you call out when initiating a go-around?
explanation: Calling "Go-around" aloud confirms your decision to the instructor, passengers, and yourself before executing the maneuver.

label: Straight and level — nose attitude reference
source: Normal procedures - Straight and level flight (p.23)
question: For straight and level flight, where should the nose be positioned relative to the horizon?
explanation: About 5 fingers below the horizon is the visual pitch reference for straight and level — a consistent sight picture for maintaining altitude.

label: Engine running roughly — first actions
value: Carburetor heat ON, electric fuel pump ON, check BOTH magnetos
question: On the ENGINE RUNNING ROUGHLY checklist, what are the first three actions?
distractors: Throttle idle, master OFF, canopy open
Flaps LDG, carb heat OFF, fuel pump OFF
explanation: Carb heat ON, electric fuel pump ON, and checking BOTH magnetos address the three most common causes of rough running — icing, fuel supply, and ignition.

label: Loss of oil pressure with normal oil temperature
source: Abnormal checklist - Loss of oil pressure (p.10)
question: If oil pressure drops below the green arc but oil temperature is normal, what should you do?
distractors: Shut down engine immediately in flight
Land as soon as possible regardless of temperature
explanation: Low oil pressure with normal temperature suggests a gauge or pump issue rather than imminent engine failure — land at the nearest airport to investigate safely.

label: Crosswind landing — which main gear touches first
question: During a crosswind landing, which main gear wheel should touch down first?
explanation: Upwind main gear first in a crosswind landing keeps the into-wind wing down and prevents weathervaning and ground looping after touchdown.

label: Departure briefing example — circuit departure point
value: 700 ft at the crosswind leg under 45°
question: In the example departure briefing, where and how is the circuit left?
distractors: 500 ft on the downwind leg at 90°
700 ft on the base leg at 30°
explanation: The example departure briefing leaves at 700 ft on the crosswind leg under 45° — the standard Hilversum circuit departure procedure.

label: Precautionary landing — initial distress call
value: PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN
question: What radio call is made at the start of a precautionary landing?
distractors: No call required for precautionary landing
explanation: PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN alerts ATC that you have an urgent situation that is not yet a full emergency — appropriate for a precautionary landing.

label: Fuel dipstick — 3rd notch
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick (p.7)
question: On the DV20 fuel dipstick, what does the third notch indicate?
explanation: The third notch on the fuel dipstick indicates 30 liters of usable fuel — a mid-range reference for preflight fuel verification.

label: Fuel dipstick — total marked capacity
value: 60 liters (77 L total capacity is not marked)
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick (p.7)
question: What is the highest fuel level marked on the DV20 fuel dipstick?
explanation: The highest dipstick marking is 60 liters, not the full 77 L capacity — you must interpolate or calculate remaining fuel above the highest notch.

label: Oil check — required oil type
source: Preflight - Instructions for checking and refilling oil (p.7)
question: What oil type does the Katana DV20 require?
explanation: Aeroshell Sport Plus 4 is the specified engine oil for the Katana DV20 — using the correct grade ensures proper lubrication and engine protection.

label: Oil check — propeller rotation direction
value: Counterclockwise (viewed from in front of the aircraft)
source: Preflight - Instructions for checking and refilling oil (p.7)
question: When turning the propeller to check oil level, in which direction must it be turned?
explanation: Turn the prop counterclockwise (from in front) during oil checks — the normal rotation direction that prevents gearbox damage.

label: Oil dipstick — min to max range
question: What is the difference between minimum and maximum on the DV20 oil dipstick?
explanation: 0.45 liters between MIN and MAX on the oil dipstick is a small quantity — even a small overfill or underfill matters for this engine.

label: COLD START — choke position
question: On a cold start, what position is the choke set to?
explanation: Choke ON for a cold start enriches the mixture for a cold engine that cannot vaporize fuel efficiently at ambient temperature.

label: AFTER STARTING ENGINE — initial throttle RPM
question: After starting the engine, to what RPM range is the throttle set?
explanation: 1100–1500 RPM after engine start allows oil pressure to stabilize and the engine to warm without excessive RPM on the ground.

label: ENGINE RUNUP — transponder mode before takeoff
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: During the engine run-up, to what mode is the transponder set?
explanation: Transponder ALT mode during run-up ensures altitude reporting is active before entering controlled or monitored airspace.

label: CRUISE — propeller speed range
question: On the CRUISE checklist, what is the propeller speed control lever range?
explanation: 1900–2400 RPM is the CRUISE checklist prop range — the normal operating band for cruise power settings.

label: APPROACH checklist — altimeter setting
question: On the APPROACH checklist, how should the altimeter be set?
explanation: SET QNH on the APPROACH checklist ensures your altimeter reads true height above sea level for the approach and landing.

label: FINAL checklist — minimum approach speed
question: What minimum approach speed does the FINAL checklist specify?
explanation: 60 kts minimum on the FINAL checklist matches the minimum approach speed — the floor for a stabilized final approach.

label: CABIN FIRE DURING FLIGHT — first actions
value: Master switch OFF, cabin heat CLOSED, cabin air OPEN
source: Abnormal checklist - Cabin fire during flight (p.10)
question: On the CABIN FIRE DURING FLIGHT checklist, what are the first three actions?
distractors: Throttle idle, fuel shut-off CLOSED, ignition OFF
Carburetor heat ON, fuel pump ON, ignition BOTH
Flaps T/O, throttle FULL, cabin heat OPEN
explanation: Master OFF, cabin heat CLOSED, and cabin air OPEN for cabin fire — remove electrical power, stop heat flow, and vent smoke from the cockpit.

label: ENGINE FIRE ON THE GROUND — throttle position
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire on the ground (p.10)
question: On the ENGINE FIRE ON THE GROUND checklist, to what position is the throttle set?
explanation: Throttle FULL during engine fire on the ground keeps the engine running to draw fire extinguishing agent through the engine bay if available, then evacuate.

label: Flap failure — only T/O available
question: If only the T/O flap position is available, what approach speed should be used?
explanation: If only T/O flaps are available, use normal approach speed — T/O flaps provide adequate drag for approach without the full landing flap configuration.

label: Flap failure — only LDG available
question: If only the LDG flap position is available, how should the landing be flown?
explanation: If only LDG flaps are available, fly a normal landing — the landing flap position is actually the preferred configuration for landing.

label: LO/V caution light — first action
question: If the LO/V caution light illuminates in flight, what is the first action?
explanation: Landing light OFF when the LO/V (low voltage) caution illuminates reduces electrical load on a potentially failing electrical system.

label: Briefing structure
question: What is the suggested structure for a departure or approach briefing?
explanation: Plan, threats, threat management, and miscellaneous — the four-part briefing structure ensures you cover the flight, risks, mitigations, and other items systematically.

label: Departure briefing — rejected takeoff item
value: What to do in case of rejected takeoff
question: Which item should a departure briefing include regarding takeoff safety?
explanation: A departure briefing must include rejected takeoff actions — knowing your abort plan before the takeoff roll is essential for safety.

label: EHHV departure — first radio call
value: Radio check only (aerodrome info obtained at havendienst visit)
question: For departure at EHHV, what does the first radio call consist of?
explanation: The first radio call at EHHV is a radio check only — aerodrome information is obtained during the havendienst (airport office) visit before flight.

label: Initial ATS contact — call sign usage
value: Always use the full call sign on initial contact
question: On initial contact with an ATS unit, which call sign must the pilot use?
distractors: No call sign required at uncontrolled fields
explanation: Full call sign on initial contact with ATS ensures positive identification — abbreviations are only used after ATC acknowledges and abbreviates first.

label: EHHV recommended circuit calls
value: Initial call and position; report downwind; report final and intention
question: What are the recommended radio calls when operating in the EHHV circuit?
explanation: EHHV circuit calls: initial call with position, downwind report, and final with intention — the standard sequence for traffic awareness at Hilversum.

label: Approved fuel — AVGAS
source: General Aircraft Information - Approved fuel grades (p.16)
question: Which aviation fuel grade is approved for the DV20?
explanation: AVGAS 100LL is an approved fuel grade for the DV20 — the standard aviation gasoline when MOGAS is not available or preferred.

label: Maximum fuel capacity in liters
question: What is the maximum fuel capacity in liters for PH-MFT and PH-SKM?
explanation: 79 liters is the maximum fuel capacity (including unusable) for PH-MFT and PH-SKM — the total tank volume, not the usable amount.

label: Fuel consumption at 75% power
source: General aircraft numbers - Average fuel usage (p.17)
question: At 75% power (2300 RPM, 25.7" MP at 2000 ft), what is the approximate fuel flow?
explanation: ±15.3 L/hr at 75% power (2300 RPM, 25.7" MP at 2000 ft) is the planning fuel flow for cruise fuel calculations.

label: Stall speed flaps T/O
question: What is the stall speed with flaps in the T/O position?
explanation: 39 kts stall speed with T/O flaps — between clean (43 kts) and full flap (38 kts), reflecting the partial flap configuration.

label: Checklist technique
value: Keep thumb by the last completed item
question: What checklist technique does the procedures document recommend?
distractors: Read the entire checklist aloud before starting
explanation: Keeping your thumb by the last completed checklist item prevents skipping steps — a simple technique for disciplined checklist flow.

label: Transition to climb — clearing turns interval
value: Every 500 ft (15° bank, 30° left and right of course)
question: During a climb, how often should clearing turns be performed?
explanation: Clearing turns every 500 ft during climb (15° bank, 30° left and right) maintains situational awareness and traffic scan during the climb.

label: Level-off from descent — power increase point
question: When leveling off from a descent, at what point is power increased to 25"/2400 RPM?
explanation: Power to 25"/2400 RPM at 50 ft before target altitude during descent level-off prevents an altitude undershoot while smoothly transitioning to cruise.

label: Level turn — roll-out trigger
question: In a level turn, when should you begin rolling wings level?
explanation: Begin rolling wings level 10° before the desired heading in a level turn — lead angle compensates for roll-out lag.

label: Climbing turn — roll-out trigger
question: In a climbing turn, when should you begin rolling wings level?
explanation: Begin rolling wings level 5° before desired heading in a climbing turn — a smaller lead than level turns because the turn rate is slower at climb speed.

label: Stall exercise — crew briefing items
value: Type of stall, altitude/heading, recovery start and manner
question: What must be briefed before a stall exercise?
distractors: Fuel quantity and passenger weights only
explanation: Pre-stall briefings must cover stall type, altitude/heading, and recovery start/manner — ensuring both pilots know the plan before the maneuver.

label: Before stall — inside checks fuel pump
question: Before a stall exercise, what position is the electric fuel pump set to?
explanation: Electric fuel pump ON before stall exercises ensures uninterrupted fuel flow during the low-power, high-AOA phases of the maneuver.

label: Approach to stall in descending turn — bank angle
source: Air exercises - Approach to stall in descending turn (p.33)
question: In the approach-to-stall exercise in a descending turn, what bank angle is used?
explanation: 20° bank in an approach-to-stall descending turn adds a turning component while still allowing controlled stall entry practice.

label: Approach stall recovery — minimum safe speed before flap retraction
source: Air exercises - Approach to stall in descending turn (p.33)
question: Recovering from an approach-to-stall in a descending turn, at what minimum speed may flaps be selected UP?
explanation: Flaps UP at minimum 60 kts during recovery from approach-to-stall in a descending turn — above stall speed with margin before retraction.

label: Sky clearing turns before stall
question: What sky-clearing turns are required before a stall exercise?
explanation: 2 × 90° or one 180° clearing turn before stall exercises ensures the area is clear of traffic before beginning the maneuver.

label: Normal takeoff — required callouts
value: Engine instruments checked (2400 RPM ±100) and airspeed alive
question: During a normal takeoff roll, what two items must be checked and called out?
distractors: Flaps UP and carb heat OFF
Transponder ALT and landing light ON
explanation: Engine instruments (2400 RPM ±100) and airspeed alive are the two critical takeoff roll callouts — confirming power and instrument function before rotation.

label: Normal takeoff — power reduction at 700 ft AAL
question: After climbing to 700 ft AAL in the circuit, what power and speed are set?
explanation: At 700 ft AAL in the circuit after takeoff, ±21" MP and 70 kts is the standard downwind configuration per the takeoff procedures.

label: Soft field takeoff — stick position during taxi
source: Takeoff procedures - Soft or rough field takeoff (p.37)
question: During taxi for a soft or rough field takeoff, where should the control stick be held?
explanation: Full aft stick during taxi for soft field takeoff keeps weight off the nosewheel, reducing drag and preventing nosewheel digging on soft surfaces.

label: Obstacle clearance takeoff — brakes technique
value: Hold brakes, advance throttle FULL, check engine instruments, then release
question: How is an obstacle clearance (short field) takeoff initiated?
explanation: Hold brakes, advance throttle FULL, check engine instruments, then release — the short field takeoff sequence that maximizes energy at rotation.

label: Enroute climb — throttle and prop settings
value: Throttle FULL OPEN, propeller 2400 RPM, Vy 70 kts
question: What are the enroute climb settings for the DV20?
explanation: Enroute climb uses full throttle, 2400 RPM, and Vy 70 kts — maximum climb performance for the clean configuration during cross-country.

label: Downwind check — throttle after flap selection
value: Immediately advance to ±24" MP to maintain 70 kts
question: On downwind after selecting flaps T/O, what throttle action maintains 70 kts?
explanation: Immediately advancing to ±24" MP after selecting T/O flaps on downwind prevents speed decay from the increased drag of extended flaps.

label: Base leg — bank angle
question: What bank angle is used when turning from downwind to base?
explanation: 15°–20° bank (max 30°) on the downwind-to-base turn gives a controlled turn rate without excessive bank close to the ground.

label: Normal landing — roundout height
question: During a normal landing, at what height above the runway do you establish level flight in the roundout?
explanation: Level flight about 1 m above the runway in the roundout is the transition point before retarding the throttle to idle for touchdown.

label: Post-landing braking caution
value: Do not brake excessively to exit sooner (risk of blown tires)
question: Why should you avoid excessive braking after landing to exit the runway sooner?
distractors: It causes the nosewheel to lift off
explanation: Excessive braking to exit sooner risks blown tires — the NOTE warns against sacrificing tire integrity for a shorter taxi distance.

label: Touch and go — RT requirement
value: Include touch-and-go intention in the final radio call
question: When planning a touch and go, when should you state your intention on the radio?
explanation: State touch-and-go intention in the final radio call so other circuit traffic knows you will not full-stop and vacate the runway.

label: Crosswind takeoff — control stick position
value: Into the wind (maximum deflection, reducing with speed)
question: During a crosswind takeoff, how should the control stick be positioned?
explanation: Control stick into the wind during crosswind takeoff (maximum deflection reducing with speed) counteracts weathervaning and keeps the wings level during the roll.

label: Simulated EFATO — controls handling
value: Do not turn anything off; point to switches (touch-drill only)
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: During a simulated engine failure after takeoff exercise, how should switches be handled?
distractors: Turn all switches off as in the real checklist
Leave the aircraft to the instructor
explanation: During simulated EFATO, do not actually switch anything off — point to switches only (touch-drill) to practice the flow without creating a real emergency.

label: Engine failure in flight — initial speed reduction
value: Maintain altitude until speed reduces to 70 kts, then lower nose to glide
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: On engine failure in flight, how do you establish the glide?
explanation: Maintain altitude until speed reduces to 70 kts, then lower the nose — the correct technique to establish best glide without diving below optimal glide speed.

label: Emergency landing — aim point on field
value: Aim for the first third of the landing field
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.47)
question: During an emergency landing approach, where on the field should you aim?
distractors: The far end of the field
explanation: Aim for the first third of the landing field — provides margin if you come up short while avoiding the far end where obstacles may exist.

label: Emergency landing — ELT action
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: During an engine failure in flight, what action is taken with the ELT?
explanation: ELT ON manually after engine failure ensures the emergency locator activates even if the automatic g-switch does not trigger.

label: Precautionary landing — downwind altitude
question: At what altitude is the downwind flown during a precautionary landing?
explanation: 500 ft downwind during a precautionary landing — lower than a normal circuit to allow closer inspection of the landing site.

label: Precautionary landing — inspection run level-off
value: 200 ft, throttle ±24" MP, 70 kts, stop descent
question: During a precautionary landing inspection run, at what height do you level off abeam the field?
explanation: Level off at 200 ft abeam the field during inspection run at ±24" MP and 70 kts — close enough to assess the surface while maintaining safe speed.

label: AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist — scenario variant
value: Select flaps UP and set propeller speed to 2400 RPM
question: You've passed 200 ft AAL on departure — what two items does the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist call for?
distractors: Set flaps T/O and reduce to idle power
Retract flaps and turn electric fuel pump OFF
Extend flaps LDG and set MAX RPM
explanation: Passing 200 ft AAL triggers the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist: flaps UP and 2400 RPM transition you from takeoff into a clean climb configuration.

label: Electric fuel pump OFF timing — recall variant
question: Climbing out in the circuit — above what height AAL should the electric fuel pump be switched OFF?
explanation: The electric fuel pump is switched OFF above 1000 ft AAL because the engine-driven pump should now supply adequate fuel pressure on its own.

label: Engine run-up — throttle RPM — chair-flying variant
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off
question: Chair-flying the run-up with brakes held — what RPM do you set on the throttle?
explanation: 1700 RPM with brakes held during run-up is the standard setting for magneto, propeller, and carburetor heat checks before takeoff.

label: Maximum delay before shutting down the engine — scenario variant
question: Oil pressure reads below 1.5 bar right after start — how long may you wait before shutting down?
explanation: If oil pressure stays below 1.5 bar after start, the CAUTION limits you to 10 seconds before shutdown to prevent engine damage from inadequate lubrication.

label: DOWNWIND checklist — recall variant
question: Running the DOWNWIND checklist — where should carburetor heat be set?
explanation: Carburetor heat ON on downwind protects against carb icing during the low-power circuit phase when throttle is typically below 20" MP.

label: BALKED LANDING checklist speed — scenario variant
question: You're executing a balked landing — what airspeed does the checklist target?
explanation: The balked landing checklist targets 58 kts (Vx) — best angle of climb to gain altitude quickly when aborting a landing.

label: Engine failure in flight — initial speed — scenario variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine failure during flight
question: The engine quits in cruise — per the abnormal checklist, what airspeed do you establish first?
explanation: After engine failure in cruise, establish 70 kts first — best glide with flaps UP — giving you control and time to plan before further actions.

label: Emergency landing engine off — approach speed — recall variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Emergency landing with engine off
question: Gliding to a forced landing with the engine off — what approach speed does the checklist specify?
explanation: 59 kts is the checklist approach speed for a dead-stick landing, balancing controllability with minimum sink rate.

label: Flap failure UP only — speed adjustment — scenario variant
question: Your flaps are stuck UP and won't extend — how should you adjust approach speed?
explanation: With flaps stuck UP, raise approach speed by 10 kts to compensate for the higher stall speed without landing flap benefit.

label: Taxi warm-up RPM — recall variant
question: Taxiing on a cold morning — what RPM range warms the oil to 50 °C?
explanation: On cold mornings, 1100–1500 RPM during taxi warms oil to 50 °C without over-revving or excessive brake use on the ground.

label: Vy (best rate of climb) — recall variant
question: You're climbing out after flap retraction — what speed gives you the best rate of climb in the DV20?
explanation: Vy of 70 kts with flaps UP is the speed that produces the greatest rate of climb in the clean configuration — more altitude per minute than Vx, which optimizes angle instead.

label: Vy with flaps T/O — scenario variant
question: Still climbing with takeoff flaps selected — what Vy should you hold?
explanation: With takeoff flaps still selected, Vy drops to 65 kts because the wing produces more lift at a lower angle of attack, changing the best rate-of-climb point.

label: Vx with flaps T/O — recall variant
question: Obstacle ahead and flaps still at T/O — what speed gives the best angle of climb?
explanation: Vx of 58 kts with flaps T/O gives the steepest climb path — use it when you need to clear an obstacle rather than maximize climb rate.

label: VS1 flaps up — memory check variant
question: Flaps fully retracted — what is VS1, the clean-configuration stall speed?
explanation: VS1 of 43 kts is the clean-configuration stall speed (flaps UP) — the baseline reference for approach margins and slow-flight training.

label: VSO flaps down — recall variant
question: Full landing flaps extended — what stall speed (VSO) should you remember?
explanation: VSO of 38 kts is the stall speed with landing flaps — the lower end of the white arc on the airspeed indicator.

label: VFE — scenario variant
question: Before selecting more flap on final — what is the maximum speed with flaps extended (VFE)?
explanation: VFE of 81 kts is the maximum speed with flaps extended; exceeding it risks structural damage to the flap system, which is why flap changes require a "Speed checked" callout below this limit.

label: VA maneuvering speed — recall variant
question: Entering turbulence or steep maneuvers — what is VA for the DV20?
explanation: VA of 104 kts is maneuvering speed — below this speed the wing will stall before exceeding the design load limit in full-deflection maneuvers.

label: Max demonstrated crosswind — scenario variant
question: ATIS reports 15 kt crosswind component — is that within the DV20's demonstrated limit?
explanation: 15 kts is the maximum demonstrated crosswind component; it represents flight-test experience, not a hard legal limit, but is the figure you plan around for safe operations.

label: Best glide flaps UP — scenario variant
question: Engine failure with flaps UP — what glide speed maximizes your range?
explanation: Best glide with flaps UP is 70 kts — the speed that maximizes range and time aloft after an engine failure in the clean configuration.

label: Best glide flaps T/O — recall variant
question: Gliding with takeoff flaps still out — what airspeed should you hold?
explanation: With takeoff flaps out, best glide is 64 kts — slightly slower than clean because the extended flaps change the wing's lift/drag characteristics.

label: MTOW PH-MFT and PH-SKM — recall variant
question: Loading the aircraft before flight — what is the maximum takeoff weight for PH-MFT and PH-SKM?
explanation: 730 kg is the maximum takeoff weight for PH-MFT and PH-SKM; exceeding it violates loading limits and degrades performance margins.

label: Usable fuel capacity — recall variant
source: General aircraft numbers - Average fuel usage (p.17)
question: Planning fuel for a cross-country — how many liters of usable fuel does the tank hold?
explanation: 77 liters is the usable fuel capacity — the quantity you can actually burn in flight, distinct from total tank capacity which includes unusable fuel.

label: Glide ratio L/Dmax — recall variant
question: Estimating glide distance after engine failure — what L/D ratio does the Katana achieve?
explanation: A 14:1 glide ratio means for every 1000 ft of altitude you can glide roughly 2.8 nm in still air — a planning figure for engine-out scenarios.

label: Straight and level power — chair-flying variant
source: Normal procedures - Straight and level flight (p.23)
question: Cruising straight and level — what MP, RPM, and speed does the procedures document specify?
explanation: 25" MP, 2400 RPM, and 90 kts is the standard straight-and-level cruise setting — a balanced power configuration for efficient level flight per the normal procedures.

label: Cruise flaps UP — table recall variant
question: Looking up the power/speed table for cruise with flaps UP — what settings apply?
explanation: The power/speed table lists ±25" MP, 2400 RPM, and 90 kts for cruise with flaps UP — the reference setting when established in level flight.

label: Descent flaps UP — table recall variant
question: Setting up a descent with flaps UP per the table — what MP, RPM, and speed?
explanation: For descent with flaps UP, the table specifies ±18" MP at 2400 RPM and 90 kts — reduced power while maintaining airspeed for a controlled descent.

label: Downwind flaps T/O throttle — scenario variant
question: Just selected flaps T/O on downwind — what throttle setting keeps you at 70 kts?
explanation: Selecting flaps T/O on downwind increases drag; advancing to ±24" MP restores the energy needed to hold 70 kts in the circuit.

label: Base leg initial throttle — scenario variant
question: Turning base in the circuit — what is the initial throttle (MP) setting?
explanation: 15" MP on base is the initial power reduction that begins slowing the aircraft from downwind speed toward final approach speed.

label: Minimum approach speed final — recall variant
source: Powersetting table / Circuit and normal landing (p.18, 39)
question: On short final, what is the minimum approach speed you must not go below?
explanation: 60 kts is the minimum approach speed on final — the floor that keeps adequate margin above stall while allowing a stabilized approach.

label: Carb heat minimum MP — recall variant
question: Power is coming down for a long descent — below what MP must carburetor heat be ON?
explanation: Below 20" MP the throttle is low enough that carburetor icing risk rises significantly, so carburetor heat must be ON per the power table note.

label: Climb flaps T/O table speed — recall variant
value: 65 kts at full throttle, PSCL full forward
question: Climbing with flaps T/O per the power table — what target speed and power settings?
explanation: Climb with flaps T/O uses full throttle and prop full forward at 65 kts — matching Vy for the takeoff flap configuration on the power/speed table.

label: Descent rate per inch MP — recall variant
question: Fine-tuning your descent — roughly how much extra descent rate does each inch of reduced MP add?
explanation: Each inch of reduced manifold pressure adds roughly 100 ft/min of descent rate — a useful rule of thumb when fine-tuning your descent profile.

label: Circuit continuation 700 ft — scenario variant
question: Staying in the circuit at 700 ft AAL — what power and speed do you set?
explanation: At 700 ft AAL in the circuit, ±21" MP and 70 kts is the standard downwind configuration — enough power to maintain circuit speed with flaps still UP.

label: Transition to climb speed — scenario variant
question: From straight and level into a climb — at what speed do you advance the throttle to FULL?
explanation: Advancing to full throttle at 70 kts during the climb transition ensures you have adequate airspeed before adding climb power, preventing excessive pitch and stall risk.

label: Level-off from climb speed — scenario variant
source: Normal procedures - Transition from climb to straight and level (p.24)
question: Topping out of a climb — at what airspeed do you reduce to 25"/2400 RPM?
explanation: At 90 kts during level-off from climb, power is reduced to 25"/2400 RPM — the standard cruise setting once the aircraft is stabilized in level flight.

label: Transition to descent carb heat — recall variant
question: About to reduce power for descent — what do you do with carburetor heat first?
explanation: Carburetor heat goes ON before reducing power for descent because the sudden power reduction creates ideal conditions for carburetor icing.

label: Level-off carb heat OFF point — recall variant
source: Normal procedures - Transition from descent to straight and level (p.24)
question: Leveling off from a descent — how far before target altitude do you turn carb heat OFF?
explanation: Turning carb heat OFF 100 ft before the target altitude during a descent level-off prevents carrying unnecessary heat into the cruise power setting.

label: Standard level turn bank — recall variant
question: Practicing a standard level turn — what bank angle does the procedures document use?
explanation: 30° bank is the standard for level turns — steep enough for reasonable turn rate while keeping g-load and stall speed increase manageable.

label: 30° level turn speed — scenario variant
question: Holding 30° of bank in a level turn — what approximate speed should you target?
explanation: In a 30° level turn, target approximately 95 kts to compensate for the increased load factor and prevent altitude loss.

label: Max bank climbing turn — recall variant
question: Turning while climbing — what is the maximum bank angle allowed?
explanation: Maximum 15° bank in a climbing turn limits the additional stall-speed rise and helps maintain Vy while turning — steeper banks bleed climb performance.

label: Steep turn power at 30° bank — scenario variant
question: Rolling into a 45° steep turn — what power adjustment do you make at 30° bank?
explanation: At 30° bank in a steep turn, increasing power by 2" compensates for the extra induced drag and load factor that would otherwise cause altitude loss.

label: Steep turn solo minimum altitude — recall variant
question: Flying solo and about to practice steep turns — what is the minimum altitude?
explanation: 2000 ft AGL minimum for solo steep turns provides recovery altitude if something goes wrong during the maneuver.

label: Traffic reporting method — recall variant
value: Clock position combined with above/below/on the horizon
source: Normal procedures - Reporting of other airplanes (p.20)
question: You spot traffic during scanflow — how does the document recommend you report its position?
explanation: Clock position plus above/below/on the horizon gives other traffic a precise, unambiguous location that both pilots can visualize quickly.

label: Clearing turns speed — scenario variant
question: Before starting maneuvers, you fly clearing turns — what speed do you hold?
explanation: 90 kts during clearing turns keeps the aircraft stable and gives good visibility while scanning before maneuvers.

label: Glide from level speed — scenario variant
question: Transitioning from straight and level into a glide — what target speed do you establish?
explanation: 70 kts is the target glide speed from straight and level — matching best glide with flaps UP for a controlled transition into engine-idle flight.

label: Slow flight speed — recall variant
question: Setting up for slow flight — what speed formula does the exercise use?
explanation: Slow flight is flown at VS + 10 kts — close enough to the stall to be realistic, but with a deliberate margin for control and recovery practice.

label: Slow flight entry power — scenario variant
question: Entering slow flight from cruise — to what MP do you initially reduce power?
explanation: Reducing to 12–15" MP on slow-flight entry bleeds energy gradually so you can stabilize at VS+10 without overshooting into a stall.

label: Approach-to-stall recovery trigger — recall variant
question: Practicing approach-to-stall — besides warning horn and buffet, what speed margin triggers recovery?
explanation: Recovery triggers include the stall warning, buffet, or reaching 5 kts above stall speed — whichever comes first ensures you recover before a full stall develops.

label: Power-on stall recovery speed — scenario variant
question: Recovering from a power-on stall — what minimum speed must you reach before raising the nose to climb?
explanation: In a power-on stall recovery, 60 kts minimum before raising the nose ensures adequate airflow and control authority to safely transition back to climb attitude.

label: Full stall solo recovery altitude — recall variant
question: Solo and planning a full stall exercise — what minimum recovery altitude is required?
explanation: 3000 ft AGL minimum for solo full stalls provides enough altitude to recover from a developed stall and any altitude loss during the maneuver.
