label: Approach-to-stall solo altitude — recall variant
question: Flying solo and briefing an approach-to-stall — what minimum recovery altitude applies?
explanation: 2000 ft AGL minimum for solo approach-to-stall exercises allows recovery before reaching an unsafe altitude, with less margin needed than a full stall.

label: Stall recovery priority — scenario variant
value: Decrease angle of attack, not altitude loss
question: The wing stalls unexpectedly — what takes priority during recovery?
explanation: Stall recovery always prioritizes reducing angle of attack — trying to save altitude before unstalling the wing can deepen the stall and worsen the situation.

label: Before lowering flaps — recall variant
value: Check speed is in the white arc and call out "Speed checked"
source: Normal procedures - Slow flight / stall (white arc call-out)
question: About to select a lower flap setting — what must you verify and call out first?
explanation: The white arc callout confirms your speed is at or below VFE before any flap change, preventing structural damage from extending flaps at too high an airspeed.

label: Slow flight flap retraction speed — scenario variant
source: Normal procedures - Slow flight, accelerate to 90 kts (p.29)
question: Accelerating out of slow flight with flaps down — by what speed must flaps be fully retracted?
explanation: Flaps must be fully retracted by 81 kts (VFE) when accelerating out of slow flight — you cannot leave flaps down above the maximum flap-extended speed.

label: Standard circuit altitude — recall variant
question: Joining the pattern at an uncontrolled field — what circuit altitude should you fly?
explanation: 700 ft AAL is the standard circuit height at uncontrolled aerodromes — low enough for efficient operations, high enough for a stable pattern.

label: Circuit area vertical extent — recall variant
question: How high vertically does the aerodrome circuit area extend?
explanation: The circuit area extends vertically to 1000 ft AAL — traffic above this height is outside the standard circuit protection volume.

label: Circuit join point — scenario variant
question: Approaching to join a standard circuit — where and at what angle do you intercept?
explanation: Joining at half-way downwind on a 90° intercept gives a predictable, non-conflicting entry that keeps you aligned with standard circuit flow.

label: Circuit departure point — recall variant
question: Leaving the aerodrome traffic pattern — at what point and angle do you normally depart?
explanation: Leaving the circuit at the 45° half-way crosswind point keeps your departure clear of downwind traffic and follows standard VFR circuit procedure.

label: Downwind HARS speed — scenario variant
source: Landing - Circuit and normal landing, HARS (p.39)
question: Running HARS on downwind — which airspeed is included in the check?
explanation: 70 kts is the downwind speed checked in HARS — the reference circuit speed before flap selection and base turn.

label: Base turn threshold position — scenario variant
question: Rolling base — where should the threshold appear relative to your wing?
explanation: With the threshold 45–60° behind the wing on base, you are at the correct position and distance for a stable base-to-final turn.

label: Base-to-final turn — scenario variant
question: Setting up the base-to-final turn — at what altitude and position relative to centerline?
explanation: Turning base-to-final at 500 ft and 20° before the extended centerline sets up a stabilized final without overshooting or cutting in too close.

label: Final centerline altitude — recall variant
question: By what altitude should you be established on the final approach centerline?
explanation: Being established on final centerline by 300 ft gives enough time to stabilize speed and glidepath before the roundout.

label: Final flap extension speed — scenario variant
question: Selecting landing flaps on final — you must be below what speed?
explanation: Flaps may only be selected below 81 kts (VFE) — the structural limit for extended flaps on the DV20.

label: Overfly signal area minimum height — recall variant
question: Overflying a circuit to read the signal area — what is the minimum height?
explanation: Overflying the circuit at a minimum of 1000 ft AAL keeps you clear of circuit traffic while allowing a safe read of the signal area.

label: Rotation speed normal takeoff — chair-flying variant
question: Chair-flying a normal takeoff — at what IAS do you rotate?
explanation: 51 kts is the normal rotation speed — the point where the wing generates enough lift to leave the ground smoothly without premature rotation.

label: Post-rotation acceleration — scenario variant
value: 65 kts (Vy flaps T/O) then 70 kts after flap retraction
question: Just rotated on a normal takeoff — what speed sequence do you fly until flaps are UP?
explanation: After rotation, accelerate to 65 kts (Vy with T/O flaps) then 70 kts after flap retraction — the standard climb speed sequence for a normal takeoff.

label: Long runway flap setting — scenario variant
question: Departing from a long hard-surface runway — which flap setting for a normal takeoff?
explanation: Flaps UP on a long hard runway minimizes drag during the takeoff roll, shortening ground run when runway length is not a constraint.

label: Short/soft runway flap setting — scenario variant
question: Grass strip under 800 m or soft surface — what flap setting for takeoff?
explanation: Flaps T/O on short (<800 m) or soft/grass runways reduces the rotation and liftoff speed, shortening ground run and improving takeoff performance.

label: Obstacle clearance rotation — scenario variant
question: Short field with trees at the end — at what speed do you rotate on an obstacle clearance takeoff?
explanation: 57 kts rotation on an obstacle clearance takeoff is slightly higher than normal to ensure positive climb performance over obstacles.

label: Obstacle clearance climb speed — recall variant
question: Clearing obstacles after a short-field takeoff — what climb speed do you hold?
explanation: 58 kts (Vx) after an obstacle clearance takeoff maximizes climb angle until obstacles are cleared, then you can accelerate to Vy.

label: Soft field nose-raise speed — scenario variant
source: Takeoff procedures - Soft or rough field takeoff (p.37)
question: Soft field takeoff — at what speed do you gently raise the nose to climb attitude?
explanation: Raising the nose gently at 60 kts on a soft field takeoff lifts the nosewheel early to reduce drag and prevent the nosewheel from digging in.

label: Takeoff roll engine instruments — recall variant
question: Rolling for takeoff — what RPM should the engine instruments show?
explanation: 2400 RPM ±100 during the takeoff roll confirms the engine is delivering full rated power — a critical check before committing to flight.

label: Electric fuel pump leaving circuit — scenario variant
source: Takeoff procedures - Normal takeoff, leaving the circuit (p.36)
question: Breaking out of the circuit on departure — what do you do with the electric fuel pump?
explanation: The electric fuel pump is switched OFF when leaving the circuit because the engine-driven pump is now providing adequate fuel pressure at cruise power.

label: Threshold minimum speed — recall variant
source: Landing - Circuit and normal landing, NOTE (p.39)
question: Crossing the threshold on a normal landing — what is the minimum acceptable speed?
explanation: 60 kts minimum over the threshold on a normal landing keeps adequate stall margin while allowing a stabilized approach in the landing configuration.

label: Short field threshold speed — scenario variant
question: Short field landing — what minimum speed may you carry over the threshold?
explanation: 55 kts minimum threshold speed on a short field landing reduces float and ground roll, maximizing available runway.

label: Flaps-up landing speeds — recall variant
question: Flying a flapless landing circuit — what speeds on downwind, base, and final?
explanation: A flaps-up landing uses 70 kts throughout the circuit because without flaps the higher stall speed requires a faster approach profile.

label: Normal landing throttle idle — scenario variant
value: Level flight about 1 m above the runway is established
question: In the roundout on a normal landing — when do you retard the throttle to idle?
distractors: During the round-out before the threshold
explanation: Retarding the throttle to idle once level flight about 1 m above the runway is established in the roundout — the standard technique for a normal landing.

label: Soft field landing technique — scenario variant
value: Keep nosewheel off as long as possible with full back pressure
question: Touching down on a soft grass strip — what technique keeps the nosewheel light?
distractors: Brake immediately after main gear touchdown
explanation: Keeping the nosewheel off with full back pressure on a soft field landing prevents the nosewheel from digging into soft surface and damaging the aircraft.

label: Short field post-nosewheel — scenario variant
question: Short field landing — once the nosewheel is down, what two actions follow?
distractors: Leave flaps LDG until clear of runway
explanation: After a short field landing, flaps UP reduces drag for braking effectiveness, and careful braking minimizes ground roll without risking tire damage.

label: Touch and go paved flaps — scenario variant
question: Planning a touch and go on pavement with adequate runway — what flap setting?
explanation: Flaps UP for a touch and go on pavement minimizes drag on the subsequent takeoff roll when runway length allows.

label: Touch and go grass flaps — scenario variant
question: Touch and go from a grass runway — which flap setting do you use?
explanation: Flaps T/O for a touch and go on grass improves takeoff performance on the softer surface where reduced ground run matters.

label: Strong crosswind landing type — scenario variant
question: Crosswind component is strong today — what landing configuration may be advisable?
explanation: A flapless landing in strong crosswind reduces the side force on the flaps and gives better roll control during the landing roll.

label: Go-around first actions — scenario variant
value: MAX RPM, throttle FULL, carburetor heat OFF
question: Unstable on short final — you decide to go around. What are the first three actions?
distractors: Flaps UP, idle power, carb heat ON
Retract all flaps, then add power
explanation: The first go-around actions are MAX RPM, full throttle, and carb heat OFF — immediately restoring full climb power.

label: Go-around flap setting — scenario variant
value: T/O after "Speed checked" within white arc
question: Power is in after a go-around — what flap setting, and what callout precedes it?
explanation: T/O flaps after "Speed checked" within the white arc gives partial flap for climb while staying below VFE during the initial go-around acceleration.

label: Go-around climb speed sequence — recall variant
value: 58 kts (Vx), then 65 kts (Vy) when clear of obstacles
question: Climbing out after a go-around — what speed do you hold first, and when do you transition?
explanation: 58 kts (Vx) initially after go-around maximizes climb angle; transition to 65 kts (Vy) once clear of obstacles for best rate of climb.

label: Go-around flaps UP height — recall variant
question: After a go-around, above what height do you select flaps UP and set 2400 RPM?
explanation: Above 200 ft AAL after go-around, flaps UP and 2400 RPM transition the aircraft into the standard climb configuration.

label: Hilversum go-around runway side — scenario variant
value: Right side of runway due to gliders/paras
question: Going around at Hilversum — which side of the runway must you stay clear of?
explanation: At Hilversum, staying clear of the right side of the runway on go-around avoids conflict with gliders and paragliders operating from that side.

label: Rejected takeoff abort point — scenario variant
question: Something goes wrong on the takeoff roll — before what point can you still safely abort?
explanation: You can only safely abort a takeoff before rotation; once airborne, the procedure and options change completely.

label: Simulated EFATO minimum altitude — recall variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: Your instructor wants to simulate an EFATO — below what height must the exercise not begin?
explanation: EFATO simulation below 300 ft AGL is prohibited because there is insufficient altitude to safely practice the maneuver and recover.

label: EFATO glide speed — scenario variant
value: 59 kts with flaps T/O, or 70 kts with flaps UP
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: Engine fails just after takeoff — what glide speed depends on your flap setting?
explanation: Glide speed after takeoff engine failure depends on flap setting: 59 kts with T/O flaps, 70 kts with flaps UP — matching the configuration-specific best glide speeds.

label: Insufficient power drill carb heat — recall variant
question: Running the insufficient engine power drill — where is carburetor heat set?
explanation: Carb heat OFF on the insufficient power drill eliminates carb icing as a cause and ensures full power delivery while troubleshooting.

label: Engine failure >1500 ft fly-to — scenario variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: Engine quits above 1500 ft — where do you aim to fly?
explanation: Above 1500 ft, fly a downwind with a 1000 ft abeam point — enough altitude to set up a normal circuit approach to a landing site.

label: Engine failure <700 ft fly-to — scenario variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: Engine failure below 700 ft — where is your best fly-to area?
explanation: Below 700 ft, fly direct final — there is insufficient altitude for a full circuit, so the most direct approach to a landing site is required.

label: Engine failure transponder — recall variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: Engine failure in flight — what transponder code do you squawk?
explanation: 7700 is the transponder code for emergency — alerting ATC and other traffic that you have an urgent situation.

label: Emergency landing engine off speed — recall variant
source: Abnormal checklist / Engine failure during flight (p.45-46)
question: Dead-stick approach to a field — what approach airspeed do you fly?
explanation: 59 kts for emergency landing with engine off matches the checklist approach speed for a controlled descent to touchdown with flaps in landing configuration.

label: Electrical fire first action — scenario variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Electrical fire including smoke in flight
question: Smoke in the cockpit from an electrical source — what is your very first action?
explanation: Master switch OFF is the first action for electrical fire — removing the power source stops the current that is feeding the fire.

label: Generator warning with discharge — scenario variant
value: Switch off all nonessential equipment and land at nearest suitable airport
source: Abnormal checklist - Generator warning light with engine running
question: Generator light on and ammeter shows discharge — what is the correct response?
distractors: Turn master switch ON and OFF repeatedly
explanation: With generator failure, shedding nonessential loads reduces electrical demand and may allow the remaining battery capacity to reach a suitable airport.

label: Engine fire in flight airspeed — recall variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire in flight
question: Working the engine fire in flight checklist — what airspeed is specified?
explanation: 70 kts during engine fire in flight maintains control while minimizing airflow that could feed the fire, per the abnormal checklist.

label: Abnormal procedures first priority — recall variant
value: Maintain airplane control — fly the airplane
question: Any abnormal situation in flight — what always comes first per the procedures introduction?
explanation: In any abnormal situation, flying the airplane and maintaining control always comes first — before checklists or troubleshooting.

label: Fuel dipstick lowest notch — recall variant
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick (p.7)
question: Dipping the tanks before flight — what does the bottom notch on the dipstick represent?
explanation: The lowest notch on the fuel dipstick marks 10 liters of usable fuel — the minimum quantity reading you should expect from a dip check.

label: EHHV preferred fuel — recall variant
source: General Aircraft Information - Approved fuel grades (p.16)
question: Fueling at Hilversum (EHHV) — what fuel grade is preferred per the procedures document?
explanation: MOGAS per BAZ-Regulation is the preferred fuel at EHHV (Hilversum), reflecting local operating approval for automotive gasoline.

label: Prohibited lubricant substitute — recall variant
source: General Aircraft Information - Approved lubricant, CAUTION (p.16)
question: The document's CAUTION on lubricants — what must never be used in place of engine oil?
distractors: SF or SG marked automotive oil
explanation: Aviation lubricant must never substitute for engine oil — it lacks the properties required for engine lubrication and can cause serious engine damage.

label: Hilversum circuit departure — briefing variant
question: Briefing a departure from Hilversum — where and how do you leave the circuit in the example?
explanation: The example departure briefing leaves the circuit at 700 ft on the crosswind leg under 45° — the standard Hilversum departure procedure.

label: TEM briefing purpose — recall variant
value: Threats that affect intended operations and how to mitigate them
question: In a threat and error management briefing — what should you identify and plan for?
explanation: TEM briefings identify threats to your operation and how to mitigate them — turning potential problems into planned responses before they occur.

label: EHHV glide-in requirements — scenario variant
value: Mandatory call, no traffic ahead, instructor only
question: Gliding in to EHHV from 1000 ft downwind — what conditions must be met?
explanation: The EHHV glide-in from 1000 ft downwind requires a mandatory call, no traffic ahead, and instructor only — it is not approved for solo students.

label: Solo student call sign — recall variant
question: Making your first solo radio call — what prefix should you use in your call sign?
explanation: Solo student pilots prefix their call sign with STUDENT so other traffic and ATC recognize they are a student and may need additional consideration.

label: Instruments/brakes check timing — scenario variant
source: Normal procedures - Instruments/brakes check during taxiing (p.19)
question: Taxiing out — when do you call out the instruments and brakes check?
explanation: Call out the instruments/brakes check after leaving the parking area, confirming systems before entering active taxiways.

label: VNE — recall variant
question: Red-line speed on the airspeed indicator — what is VNE for the DV20?
explanation: VNE of 161 kts is the never-exceed speed — the red line on the airspeed indicator that must never be exceeded in any condition.

label: VNO — recall variant
question: Green arc upper limit in smooth air — what is VNO?
explanation: VNO of 117 kts is the maximum structural cruising speed in smooth air — above this, only fly in smooth conditions or reduce speed.

label: Maximum baggage — recall variant
question: Loading bags in the aft compartment — what is the maximum baggage allowance?
explanation: 20 kg maximum baggage in the aft compartment keeps the CG within limits and prevents overloading the rear of the aircraft.

label: Empty weight PH-MFT — recall variant
question: Checking the W&B sheet for PH-MFT — what is its empty weight?
explanation: 511 kg is the empty weight of PH-MFT — the baseline for weight and balance calculations before adding fuel, occupants, and baggage.

label: G-load flaps extended — recall variant
question: Maneuvering with flaps extended — what G-load range is permitted in the normal category?
explanation: With flaps extended, the normal category G-load range is 0.0G to +2.0G — reduced from the clean configuration due to the lower structural limit with flaps down.

label: Circuit speed flaps UP — table recall variant
question: Flying the circuit with flaps UP per the power table — what speed and MP?
explanation: The circuit with flaps UP uses 75 kts at ±21" MP per the power table — slightly faster than the 70 kts used with T/O flaps because clean configuration has a higher stall speed.

label: Propeller RPM drop check — recall variant
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: Cycling the prop during run-up — what RPM drop confirms the governor is working?
explanation: A 100–200 RPM drop during the propeller check confirms the governor is functioning and can control prop pitch at run-up RPM.

label: Magneto check limits — recall variant
value: Max 150 RPM drop, max 50 RPM difference between L and R
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: Mag check at 1500 RPM — what RPM drop limits are acceptable on each side and between them?
distractors: Max 30 RPM drop, max 100 RPM difference
Max 200 RPM drop, max 50 RPM difference
No drop allowed on either magneto
explanation: Mag check limits of max 150 RPM drop per side and max 50 RPM difference between L and R confirm both magnetos and the ignition system are healthy.

label: Carb heat check RPM drop — recall variant
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: Carb heat check at 1500 RPM during run-up — what RPM drop confirms it is functioning?
explanation: A 30 RPM drop during the carb heat check confirms heated air is reaching the carburetor — proving the carb heat system works before flight.

label: AFTER LANDING flaps — scenario variant
question: Clear of the runway and running AFTER LANDING — what flap position do you select?
explanation: Flaps UP on the AFTER LANDING checklist reduces drag and prepares the aircraft for taxi — flaps are not needed on the ground.

label: AFTER LANDING carb heat — scenario variant
question: Taxiing clear after landing — where does carburetor heat go on the AFTER LANDING checklist?
explanation: Carb heat OFF after landing because full power is no longer needed and carb icing risk is minimal during taxi with intermittent power changes.

label: Fuel gauge ¼ tank action — scenario variant
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick and fuel gauge (p.7)
question: Fuel gauge reads one-quarter tank in flight — what does the document require?
distractors: Continue flight for up to 1 hour
Switch to electric fuel pump only
explanation: At ¼ tank on the fuel gauge, land within 30 minutes — the gauge reads optimistically, so this is a conservative fuel reserve trigger.

label: Level-off nose lowering point — recall variant
source: Normal procedures - Transition from climb to straight and level (p.24)
question: Approaching your cruise altitude from below — when do you start lowering the nose?
explanation: Begin lowering the nose 20 ft before the target altitude during climb level-off — a small lead that prevents overshooting the assigned level.

label: Glide to level transition — recall variant
source: Normal procedures - Transition from glide to straight and level (p.26)
question: Leveling off from a glide — how far before target altitude do you add power and turn carb heat OFF?
explanation: 150 ft before target altitude during glide level-off gives time to add power and turn carb heat OFF smoothly without an altitude overshoot.

label: Engine clearing during glide — recall variant
source: Normal procedures - Clearing the engine during a glide (p.25)
question: Clearing the engine on a prolonged glide — how long do you apply full power?
explanation: 3–5 seconds of full power during a prolonged glide clears spark plug fouling from the rich mixture at idle — keeping the engine ready for a power addition.

label: Scanflow engine check interval — recall variant
question: Cruising level and running your scanflow — how often should you check engine instruments and fuel?
explanation: Engine instruments and fuel quantity checked every 15 minutes during cruise catches developing problems early while they are still manageable.

label: Descending turn bank — recall variant
question: Turning while descending — what bank angle does the procedures document specify?
explanation: 30° bank in a descending turn matches the standard level turn bank angle while allowing a controlled descent path.

label: Slow flight solo altitude — recall variant
question: Solo slow flight practice — what minimum altitude must you maintain?
explanation: 2000 ft AGL minimum for solo slow flight provides recovery margin if control degrades near the stall.

label: Slow flight power at VS+10 — scenario variant
question: Holding slow flight at VS+10 — what MP range maintains the maneuver?
explanation: 22–24" MP maintains slow flight at VS+10 — enough power to hold altitude near the stall without accelerating away from the maneuver.

label: Full stall solo altitude — recall variant
question: Before a solo full stall exercise — what minimum recovery altitude is required?
explanation: 3000 ft AGL minimum for solo full stalls — same as the general full-stall solo minimum, providing adequate recovery altitude.

label: Overtaking in circuit — recall variant
question: You're faster than traffic ahead in the circuit — may you overtake within the pattern?
distractors: Permitted on the downwind leg only
Permitted if faster aircraft announces on radio
explanation: Overtaking within the circuit is not permitted — it disrupts standard circuit spacing and creates collision risk in a confined traffic pattern.

label: Flap retraction height — recall variant
question: Normal takeoff climb — at what minimum height may you select flaps UP?
explanation: Flaps UP at minimum 200 ft AAL after takeoff follows the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist — retracting flaps once safely airborne and clear of obstacles.

label: Short runway threshold 800 m — recall variant
question: Runway is 750 m — should you use flaps T/O for a normal takeoff?
explanation: Below 800 m runway length, use flaps T/O for takeoff — the shorter ground run from the lower liftoff speed is essential on limited runway.

label: Engine failure 700–1500 ft fly-to — scenario variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: Engine failure between 700 ft and 1500 ft — where do you aim to fly?
explanation: Between 700 and 1500 ft after engine failure, fly direct base — enough altitude for a base leg but not a full downwind circuit.

label: Rejected takeoff callout — scenario variant
question: Aborting the takeoff roll — what do you call out?
explanation: Call STOP during a rejected takeoff to alert your instructor and confirm the abort decision.

label: EFATO landing site angle — scenario variant
value: 30° left or right from the nose
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: Choosing a landing site after EFATO — within what angle from the nose should it lie?
explanation: Choose a landing site within 30° left or right of the nose after EFATO — you cannot turn back, so the field must be within your forward arc.

label: Crash drill airspeed — scenario variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight, crash drill (p.47)
question: Practicing the crash drill on final — what airspeed do you maintain?
explanation: 59 kts during the crash drill on final matches the emergency approach speed — practicing the speed you would fly on an actual forced landing.

label: Loss of fuel pressure first action — scenario variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Loss of fuel pressure (p.10)
question: Fuel pressure warning light comes on — what is the first checklist action?
explanation: Electric fuel pump ON is the first action for loss of fuel pressure — the backup pump restores fuel flow while you diagnose the cause.

label: Engine fire in flight flaps — recall variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire in flight (p.10)
question: Engine fire in flight checklist — what flap setting is specified?
explanation: T/O flaps during engine fire in flight provide partial flap for a controlled approach speed without the full drag of landing flaps.

label: Normal landing glidepath — recall variant
question: On final for a normal landing — what glidepath angle should you aim for?
explanation: A 3° glidepath on final is the standard approach angle — it provides a stabilized descent profile to the threshold.

label: Precautionary inspection run timing — scenario variant
source: Precautionary landing - Inspection run timing table (p.50)
question: Precautionary landing inspection run at 70 kt groundspeed — what timing checks minimal landing distance?
explanation: 14 seconds at 70 kts ground speed during a precautionary landing inspection run corresponds to the minimal landing distance check per the timing table.

label: Go-around initial callout — scenario variant
question: You commit to a go-around — what is the first thing you say aloud?
explanation: Announcing "Go-around" aloud confirms your decision before executing the maneuver.

label: Straight and level nose attitude — chair-flying variant
source: Normal procedures - Straight and level flight (p.23)
question: Chair-flying straight and level — where should the nose sit relative to the horizon?
explanation: About 5 fingers below the horizon is the visual pitch reference for straight and level — a consistent sight picture for maintaining altitude.

label: Engine running roughly first actions — scenario variant
value: Carburetor heat ON, electric fuel pump ON, check BOTH magnetos
question: Engine starts running rough in cruise — what three actions does the checklist call for first?
distractors: Throttle idle, master OFF, canopy open
Flaps LDG, carb heat OFF, fuel pump OFF
explanation: Carb heat ON, electric fuel pump ON, and checking BOTH magnetos address the three most common causes of rough running — icing, fuel supply, and ignition.

label: Low oil pressure normal temp — scenario variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Loss of oil pressure (p.10)
question: Oil pressure drops below the green arc but temperature looks normal — what do you do?
distractors: Shut down engine immediately in flight
Land as soon as possible regardless of temperature
explanation: Low oil pressure with normal temperature suggests a gauge or pump issue rather than imminent engine failure — land at the nearest airport to investigate safely.

label: Crosswind landing gear touchdown — scenario variant
question: Crosswind landing in a crab-and-kick — which main wheel should touch first?
explanation: Upwind main gear first in a crosswind landing keeps the into-wind wing down and prevents weathervaning and ground looping after touchdown.

label: Departure briefing circuit exit — recall variant
value: 700 ft at the crosswind leg under 45°
question: Example departure briefing — at what point and angle do you leave the circuit?
distractors: 500 ft on the downwind leg at 90°
700 ft on the base leg at 30°
explanation: The example departure briefing leaves at 700 ft on the crosswind leg under 45° — the standard Hilversum circuit departure procedure.

label: Precautionary landing radio call — scenario variant
value: PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN
question: Unsure about a landing site and setting up a precautionary landing — what do you transmit first?
distractors: No call required for precautionary landing
explanation: PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN PAN is the urgency call when setting up a precautionary landing — urgent but not yet a full Mayday emergency.

label: Fuel dipstick 3rd notch — recall variant
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick (p.7)
question: Reading the fuel dipstick — what usable fuel quantity does the third notch represent?
explanation: The third notch on the fuel dipstick indicates 30 liters of usable fuel — a mid-range reference for preflight fuel verification.

label: Fuel dipstick max marking — recall variant
value: 60 liters (77 L total capacity is not marked)
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick (p.7)
question: The dipstick only has a few notches — what is the highest fuel level actually marked?
explanation: The highest dipstick marking is 60 liters, not the full 77 L capacity — you must interpolate or calculate remaining fuel above the highest notch.

label: Required oil type — recall variant
source: Preflight - Instructions for checking and refilling oil (p.7)
question: Topping up engine oil on the Katana — what type does the POH require?
explanation: Aeroshell Sport Plus 4 is the specified engine oil for the Katana DV20 — using the correct grade ensures proper lubrication and engine protection.

label: Propeller rotation for oil check — recall variant
value: Counterclockwise (viewed from in front of the aircraft)
source: Preflight - Instructions for checking and refilling oil (p.7)
question: Checking oil level by turning the prop — which direction must you rotate it?
explanation: Turn the prop counterclockwise (from in front) during oil checks — the normal rotation direction that prevents gearbox damage.

label: Oil dipstick min-max range — recall variant
question: How much oil volume separates MIN and MAX on the dipstick?
explanation: 0.45 liters between MIN and MAX on the oil dipstick is a small quantity — even a small overfill or underfill matters for this engine.

label: COLD START choke — scenario variant
question: First flight of a cold morning — where does the choke go for engine start?
explanation: Choke ON for a cold start enriches the mixture for a cold engine that cannot vaporize fuel efficiently at ambient temperature.

label: AFTER STARTING ENGINE RPM — scenario variant
question: Engine is running after start — what throttle RPM range do you set?
explanation: 1100–1500 RPM after engine start allows oil pressure to stabilize and the engine to warm without excessive RPM on the ground.

label: Run-up transponder mode — recall variant
source: Normal checklist - Engine runup / before take off (p.9)
question: Engine run-up complete — what transponder mode do you select before takeoff?
explanation: Transponder ALT mode during run-up ensures altitude reporting is active before entering controlled or monitored airspace.

label: CRUISE prop range — recall variant
question: Running the CRUISE checklist — what propeller speed range is permitted?
explanation: 1900–2400 RPM is the CRUISE checklist prop range — the normal operating band for cruise power settings.

label: APPROACH altimeter — scenario variant
question: Inbound and running APPROACH — how do you set the altimeter?
explanation: SET QNH on the APPROACH checklist ensures your altimeter reads true height above sea level for the approach and landing.

label: FINAL minimum speed — scenario variant
question: On short final running the FINAL checklist — what minimum approach speed is specified?
explanation: 60 kts minimum on the FINAL checklist matches the minimum approach speed — the floor for a stabilized final approach.

label: Cabin fire first actions — scenario variant
value: Master switch OFF, cabin heat CLOSED, cabin air OPEN
source: Abnormal checklist - Cabin fire during flight (p.10)
question: Smoke from a cabin fire in flight — what three actions come first on the checklist?
distractors: Throttle idle, fuel shut-off CLOSED, ignition OFF
Carburetor heat ON, fuel pump ON, ignition BOTH
Flaps T/O, throttle FULL, cabin heat OPEN
explanation: Master OFF, cabin heat CLOSED, and cabin air OPEN for cabin fire — remove electrical power, stop heat flow, and vent smoke from the cockpit.

label: Engine fire on ground throttle — scenario variant
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire on the ground (p.10)
question: Engine fire on the ground during run-up — where does the throttle go?
explanation: Throttle FULL during engine fire on the ground keeps the engine running to draw fire extinguishing agent through the engine bay if available, then evacuate.

label: Flap failure T/O only speed — scenario variant
question: Flaps stuck at T/O and won't go to LDG — what approach speed do you use?
explanation: If only T/O flaps are available, use normal approach speed — T/O flaps provide adequate drag for approach without the full landing flap configuration.

label: Flap failure LDG only — scenario variant
question: Only LDG flap position works — how do you fly the landing?
explanation: If only LDG flaps are available, fly a normal landing — the landing flap position is actually the preferred configuration for landing.

label: LO/V caution light — scenario variant
question: LO/V caution light illuminates in flight — what is the first action?
explanation: Landing light OFF when the LO/V (low voltage) caution illuminates reduces electrical load on a potentially failing electrical system.

label: Briefing structure — recall variant
question: Structuring a departure or approach briefing — what four topics does the document suggest?
explanation: Plan, threats, threat management, and miscellaneous — the four-part briefing structure ensures you cover the flight, risks, mitigations, and other items systematically.

label: Departure briefing RTO — recall variant
value: What to do in case of rejected takeoff
question: Pre-takeoff briefing — what takeoff-safety item must you cover?
explanation: A departure briefing must include rejected takeoff actions — knowing your abort plan before the takeoff roll is essential for safety.

label: EHHV first radio call — recall variant
value: Radio check only (aerodrome info obtained at havendienst visit)
question: Keying the mic at EHHV for the first time today — what does that call consist of?
explanation: The first radio call at EHHV is a radio check only — aerodrome information is obtained during the havendienst (airport office) visit before flight.

label: Initial ATS call sign — recall variant
value: Always use the full call sign on initial contact
question: First contact with approach control — how much of your call sign do you use?
distractors: No call sign required at uncontrolled fields
explanation: Full call sign on initial contact with ATS ensures positive identification — abbreviations are only used after ATC acknowledges and abbreviates first.

label: EHHV circuit calls — recall variant
value: Initial call and position; report downwind; report final and intention
question: Operating in the EHHV circuit — what radio calls does the AIP recommend?
explanation: EHHV circuit calls: initial call with position, downwind report, and final with intention — the standard sequence for traffic awareness at Hilversum.

label: Approved AVGAS grade — recall variant
source: General Aircraft Information - Approved fuel grades (p.16)
question: Fueling with aviation gasoline — which grade is approved for the DV20?
explanation: AVGAS 100LL is an approved fuel grade for the DV20 — the standard aviation gasoline when MOGAS is not available or preferred.

label: Max fuel capacity liters — recall variant
question: Calculating fuel for PH-MFT or PH-SKM — what is the maximum tank capacity in liters?
explanation: 79 liters is the maximum fuel capacity (including unusable) for PH-MFT and PH-SKM — the total tank volume, not the usable amount.

label: 75% power fuel flow — recall variant
source: General aircraft numbers - Average fuel usage (p.17)
question: Cruising at 75% power (2300 RPM, 25.7" MP at 2000 ft) — what fuel flow should you expect?
explanation: ±15.3 L/hr at 75% power (2300 RPM, 25.7" MP at 2000 ft) is the planning fuel flow for cruise fuel calculations.

label: Stall speed flaps T/O — recall variant
question: Takeoff flaps selected during slow flight — what stall speed applies?
explanation: 39 kts stall speed with T/O flaps — between clean (43 kts) and full flap (38 kts), reflecting the partial flap configuration.

label: Checklist technique — recall variant
value: Keep thumb by the last completed item
question: Working through a checklist in flight — where should your thumb stay?
distractors: Read the entire checklist aloud before starting
explanation: Keeping your thumb by the last completed checklist item prevents skipping steps — a simple technique for disciplined checklist flow.

label: Climb clearing turns — recall variant
value: Every 500 ft (15° bank, 30° left and right of course)
question: Climbing on course — how often and how do you perform clearing turns?
explanation: Clearing turns every 500 ft during climb (15° bank, 30° left and right) maintains situational awareness and traffic scan during the climb.

label: Level-off from descent power — recall variant
question: Leveling off from a descent — when do you advance power to 25"/2400 RPM?
explanation: Power to 25"/2400 RPM at 50 ft before target altitude during descent level-off prevents an altitude undershoot while smoothly transitioning to cruise.

label: Level turn roll-out — recall variant
question: Rolling out of a level turn — how far before your target heading do you start leveling the wings?
explanation: Begin rolling wings level 10° before the desired heading in a level turn — lead angle compensates for roll-out lag.

label: Climbing turn roll-out — recall variant
question: Rolling out of a climbing turn — when do you begin wings level?
explanation: Begin rolling wings level 5° before desired heading in a climbing turn — a smaller lead than level turns because the turn rate is slower at climb speed.

label: Stall exercise briefing — recall variant
value: Type of stall, altitude/heading, recovery start and manner
question: Before a stall exercise with your instructor — what must the briefing cover?
distractors: Fuel quantity and passenger weights only
explanation: Pre-stall briefings must cover stall type, altitude/heading, and recovery start/manner — ensuring both pilots know the plan before the maneuver.

label: Before stall fuel pump — recall variant
question: Setting up for a stall exercise — where is the electric fuel pump?
explanation: Electric fuel pump ON before stall exercises ensures uninterrupted fuel flow during the low-power, high-AOA phases of the maneuver.

label: Approach stall descending turn bank — scenario variant
source: Air exercises - Approach to stall in descending turn (p.33)
question: Approach-to-stall in a descending turn — what bank angle is used?
explanation: 20° bank in an approach-to-stall descending turn adds a turning component while still allowing controlled stall entry practice.

label: Approach stall flap retraction speed — scenario variant
source: Air exercises - Approach to stall in descending turn (p.33)
question: Recovering from approach-to-stall in a descending turn — at what minimum speed may flaps go UP?
explanation: Flaps UP at minimum 60 kts during recovery from approach-to-stall in a descending turn — above stall speed with margin before retraction.

label: Sky clearing turns before stall — recall variant
question: Before starting a stall exercise — what clearing turns are required?
explanation: 2 × 90° or one 180° clearing turn before stall exercises ensures the area is clear of traffic before beginning the maneuver.

label: Normal takeoff roll callouts — scenario variant
value: Engine instruments checked (2400 RPM ±100) and airspeed alive
question: Accelerating down the runway — what two checks do you call out during the roll?
distractors: Flaps UP and carb heat OFF
Transponder ALT and landing light ON
explanation: Engine instruments (2400 RPM ±100) and airspeed alive are the two critical takeoff roll callouts — confirming power and instrument function before rotation.

label: 700 ft circuit power — scenario variant
question: Reaching 700 ft AAL in the circuit after takeoff — what power and speed do you set?
explanation: At 700 ft AAL in the circuit after takeoff, ±21" MP and 70 kts is the standard downwind configuration per the takeoff procedures.

label: Soft field taxi stick — scenario variant
source: Takeoff procedures - Soft or rough field takeoff (p.37)
question: Taxiing for a soft-field takeoff — where do you hold the stick?
explanation: Full aft stick during taxi for soft field takeoff keeps weight off the nosewheel, reducing drag and preventing nosewheel digging on soft surfaces.

label: Obstacle clearance brake technique — chair-flying variant
value: Hold brakes, advance throttle FULL, check engine instruments, then release
question: Chair-flying a short-field takeoff — what is the brake and throttle sequence?
explanation: Hold brakes, advance throttle FULL, check engine instruments, then release — the short field takeoff sequence that maximizes energy at rotation.

label: Enroute climb settings — recall variant
value: Throttle FULL OPEN, propeller 2400 RPM, Vy 70 kts
question: Climbing enroute to your destination — what throttle, prop, and speed settings apply?
explanation: Enroute climb uses full throttle, 2400 RPM, and Vy 70 kts — maximum climb performance for the clean configuration during cross-country.

label: Downwind throttle after flaps — scenario variant
value: Immediately advance to ±24" MP to maintain 70 kts
question: Flaps just went to T/O on downwind and speed is dropping — what throttle action restores 70 kts?
explanation: Immediately advancing to ±24" MP after selecting T/O flaps on downwind prevents speed decay from the increased drag of extended flaps.

label: Downwind-to-base bank — scenario variant
question: Turning from downwind to base — what bank angle should you use?
explanation: 15°–20° bank (max 30°) on the downwind-to-base turn gives a controlled turn rate without excessive bank close to the ground.

label: Roundout height — scenario variant
question: Flaring for a normal landing — at what height above the runway do you establish the roundout?
explanation: Level flight about 1 m above the runway in the roundout is the transition point before retarding the throttle to idle for touchdown.

label: Excessive braking caution — recall variant
value: Do not brake excessively to exit sooner (risk of blown tires)
question: Tempted to brake hard to make the first taxiway — why does the document warn against it?
distractors: It causes the nosewheel to lift off
explanation: Excessive braking to exit sooner risks blown tires — the NOTE warns against sacrificing tire integrity for a shorter taxi distance.

label: Touch and go radio call — scenario variant
value: Include touch-and-go intention in the final radio call
question: Planning a touch and go — when must you announce your intention on the radio?
explanation: State touch-and-go intention in the final radio call so other circuit traffic knows you will not full-stop and vacate the runway.

label: Crosswind takeoff stick — scenario variant
value: Into the wind (maximum deflection, reducing with speed)
question: Takeoff roll with a strong crosswind — how do you position the control stick?
explanation: Control stick into the wind during crosswind takeoff (maximum deflection reducing with speed) counteracts weathervaning and keeps the wings level during the roll.

label: Simulated EFATO controls — recall variant
value: Do not turn anything off; point to switches (touch-drill only)
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure after takeoff (p.45)
question: Instructor simulates EFATO — may you actually switch anything off?
distractors: Turn all switches off as in the real checklist
Leave the aircraft to the instructor
explanation: During simulated EFATO, do not actually switch anything off — point to switches only (touch-drill) to practice the flow without creating a real emergency.

label: Engine failure glide establishment — scenario variant
value: Maintain altitude until speed reduces to 70 kts, then lower nose to glide
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: Engine quits at altitude — how do you transition into the best glide?
explanation: Maintain altitude until speed reduces to 70 kts, then lower the nose — the correct technique to establish best glide without diving below optimal glide speed.

label: Emergency landing aim point — scenario variant
value: Aim for the first third of the landing field
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.47)
question: Setting up a forced landing approach — where on the field should you aim to touch down?
distractors: The far end of the field
explanation: Aim for the first third of the landing field — provides margin if you come up short while avoiding the far end where obstacles may exist.

label: Emergency landing ELT — scenario variant
source: Abnormal procedures - Engine failure during flight (p.46)
question: Engine failure in flight — what do you do with the ELT?
explanation: ELT ON manually after engine failure ensures the emergency locator activates even if the automatic g-switch does not trigger.

label: Precautionary downwind altitude — scenario variant
question: Flying a precautionary landing pattern — at what altitude is downwind flown?
explanation: 500 ft downwind during a precautionary landing — lower than a normal circuit to allow closer inspection of the landing site.

label: Precautionary inspection run level-off — scenario variant
value: 200 ft, throttle ±24" MP, 70 kts, stop descent
question: Abeam the field on a precautionary landing inspection run — at what height and settings do you level off?
explanation: Level off at 200 ft abeam the field during inspection run at ±24" MP and 70 kts — close enough to assess the surface while maintaining safe speed.

label: WARM START — throttle and choke
value: Throttle approximately 2 cm forward, choke OFF
question: On a WARM START, how should the throttle and choke be set?
explanation: On a warm start the engine is already warm, so choke stays OFF and throttle is set approximately 2 cm forward for a leaner starting mixture.

label: Full stall — dual minimum recovery altitude
question: When flying dual, what is the minimum recovery altitude for a full stall exercise?
explanation: When flying dual, 2000 ft AGL is the minimum recovery altitude for a full stall — the instructor can take over if recovery is needed, so less solo margin is required.

label: Approach-to-stall — dual minimum recovery altitude
question: When flying dual, what is the minimum recovery altitude for an approach-to-stall exercise?
explanation: When flying dual, 1500 ft AGL suffices for approach-to-stall — less altitude needed than solo because the instructor provides a safety backup.

label: Stall exercise prep — landing light
question: Before a stall exercise, what position should the landing light be in?
explanation: Landing light ON before stall exercises improves visibility of the wing and horizon reference during the maneuver.

label: APOS — open water restriction
value: Do not fly over open water (lack of horizon)
question: For stall exercise position (APOS), over which terrain should you NOT fly because of lack of horizon?
distractors: Small lakes with a visible shoreline
explanation: Avoid flying stall exercises over open water — the lack of a visible horizon makes attitude reference difficult and can disorient the pilot.

label: Stall recovery without power — target speed
question: In a stall recovery WITHOUT power, to what attitude and speed do you transition?
explanation: Stall recovery without power transitions to glide attitude at 70 kts — best glide speed to maximize range and control after an unpowered stall recovery.

label: Low ceiling / visibility circuit — weather limit
value: Do not continue into marginal VFR weather
source: Landing - Circuit with low ceilings / visibility weather (p.43)
question: When flying a circuit with low ceilings or reduced visibility, what should you avoid doing?
explanation: Do not continue into marginal VFR weather in the circuit — if ceilings or visibility are deteriorating, divert or land rather than pressing into unsafe conditions.

label: Turbulent weather circuit — flap consideration
value: Consider reduced flaps (UP or T/O) for greater controllability
question: In a turbulent-weather circuit, what flap setting should you consider for greater controllability?
distractors: Full LDG flaps throughout the circuit
Flaps UP only on final approach
No change from the normal full-flap circuit
explanation: Reduced flaps (UP or T/O) in turbulent circuit conditions give greater roll control authority — full landing flaps can be harder to manage in gusty conditions.

label: Crosswind takeoff — rotation speed
value: Rotate at a slightly higher airspeed than normal
question: During a crosswind takeoff, how should rotation speed compare to a normal takeoff?
distractors: Rotate at a lower airspeed than normal
Use exactly the same rotation speed regardless of wind
Delay rotation until Vy is reached
explanation: Slightly higher rotation speed in crosswind takeoff provides additional control margin and reduces weathervaning tendency during the critical rotation phase.

label: Engine fire on the ground — final action
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire on the ground (p.10)
question: After completing the ENGINE FIRE ON THE GROUND checklist actions, what is the final step?
distractors: Wait in the cockpit for rescue
explanation: After completing engine fire on the ground checklist actions, evacuate immediately — fire can spread rapidly and the priority is getting away from the aircraft.

label: Emergency landing engine off — seat belts
source: Abnormal checklist - Emergency landing with engine off (p.10)
question: On the EMERGENCY LANDING WITH ENGINE OFF checklist, how should seat belts be set?
explanation: Seat belts TIGHT for emergency landing with engine off — securing occupants for the potentially firm touchdown of a forced landing.

label: Loss of oil pressure with rising oil temperature
value: Land as soon as possible and be prepared for engine failure and emergency landing
source: Abnormal checklist - Loss of oil pressure (p.10)
question: If oil pressure drops below the green arc AND oil temperature is rising, what should you do?
distractors: Continue to destination at reduced power
Land at the nearest airport only if oil temperature is normal
Shut down the engine immediately in flight
explanation: Low oil pressure AND rising oil temperature indicates imminent engine failure — land as soon as possible and prepare for a forced landing.

label: Engine fire in flight — electric fuel pump
source: Abnormal checklist - Engine fire in flight (p.10)
question: On the ENGINE FIRE IN FLIGHT checklist, what is the electric fuel pump setting?
explanation: Electric fuel pump OFF during engine fire in flight — removing a potential fuel source that could feed the fire.

label: Fuel gauge versus dipstick accuracy
value: The gauge shows more fuel than the dipstick (false positive)
source: Preflight - Instructions for use fuel dipstick and fuel gauge (p.7)
question: How does the cockpit fuel gauge reading compare to the fuel dipstick?
distractors: The gauge shows less fuel than the dipstick
explanation: The cockpit fuel gauge reads higher than the dipstick (false positive) — always trust the dipstick for accurate fuel quantity, not the gauge.

label: Hot engine oil dipstick reading
value: Minimum on the dipstick means the oil level is sufficient
question: If the oil dipstick shows minimum after a recently running (hot) engine, what does that indicate?
distractors: Shut down and turn the propeller before checking again
Ground the aircraft until maintenance refills oil
explanation: Minimum on the dipstick after a hot engine means sufficient oil — oil expands when hot, so the reading is lower than when cold but still adequate.

label: Propeller rotation direction — gearbox damage
value: Turning against normal rotation can damage the gearbox
question: What happens if you turn the propeller against its normal rotation direction during an oil check?
distractors: Nothing harmful if the ignition key is removed
Only the oil seal is affected
explanation: Turning the prop against normal rotation can damage the gearbox — always rotate counterclockwise when viewed from in front of the aircraft.

label: Oil check — throttle fully open safety step
value: To ensure the engine cannot start
source: Preflight - Instructions for checking and refilling oil (p.7)
question: When manually turning the propeller to check oil level, why is the throttle set fully open?
distractors: To engage the parking brake interlock
explanation: Throttle fully open during manual prop rotation ensures the engine cannot start — a critical safety step before turning the propeller by hand.

label: Approach-to-stall in climbing turn without flaps — power reduction
source: Air exercises - Approach to stall in climbing turn without flaps (p.33)
question: In the approach-to-stall exercise in a climbing turn WITHOUT flaps, to what power setting is the throttle reduced?
explanation: 18 in. MP for approach-to-stall in a climbing turn without flaps — a moderate power reduction that allows controlled deceleration toward the stall in a turn.

label: Steep turn — roll-out trigger
question: In a 45° steep turn, when should you begin rolling wings level?
explanation: Begin rolling wings level 20° before desired heading in a 45° steep turn — a larger lead angle because the turn rate is higher at steep bank.

label: Precautionary landing — aircraft condition requirement
value: The airplane must be in good technical condition
question: Before attempting a precautionary landing, what aircraft condition does the document require?
explanation: The airplane must be in good technical condition before a precautionary landing — a compromised aircraft makes an already challenging maneuver even riskier.

label: Precautionary landing inspection run timing at 60 kts
source: Precautionary landing - Inspection run timing table (p.50)
question: During a precautionary landing inspection run at 60 kts ground speed, how many seconds correspond to the minimal landing distance check?
explanation: 16 seconds at 60 kts ground speed during inspection run corresponds to the minimal landing distance check — slower speed, longer time for the same distance.

label: Empty weight PH-SKM
question: What is the empty weight of PH-SKM?
explanation: 495 kg is the empty weight of PH-SKM — used as the W&B baseline for that specific aircraft registration.

label: Unusable fuel capacity
source: General aircraft numbers - Average fuel usage (p.17)
question: What is the unusable fuel capacity of the DV20 tanks?
explanation: 2 liters unusable fuel cannot be drawn from the tanks — subtract from total capacity to get the 77 liters of usable fuel for flight planning.

label: Normal takeoff — propeller at 200 ft AAL
question: During a normal takeoff, at minimum 200 ft AAL what propeller speed setting is selected?
explanation: 2400 RPM at minimum 200 ft AAL after takeoff sets the prop for the standard climb power setting per the AFTER TAKE-OFF checklist.

label: Slow flight entry — carb heat and fuel pump
value: Carburetor heat ON, electric fuel pump ON
question: When entering slow flight, what positions are carburetor heat and the electric fuel pump set to?
distractors: Carburetor heat OFF, electric fuel pump ON
Carburetor heat ON, electric fuel pump OFF
explanation: Carb heat ON and electric fuel pump ON when entering slow flight — carb heat prevents icing at low power, and the electric pump ensures fuel flow near the stall.

label: After start — oil pressure timing
question: After starting the engine, within what time should oil pressure be in the green range?
distractors: Before advancing the throttle at all
explanation: Oil pressure must be in the green within 10 seconds after start — if not, the CAUTION requires shutdown within 10 seconds to protect the engine.
